# 大纲规划任务

项目仓库: laforest/FPGADesignElements
项目名: FPGADesignElements
讲义目录: FPGADesignElements-tutorial/
模式: full
当前 HEAD: 2450a548eeee2a4dc1c06878b7c00617dd94e2a8
代码永久链接 base: https://github.com/laforest/FPGADesignElements/blob/2450a548eeee2a4dc1c06878b7c00617dd94e2a8/
用户关注点: （无）


---

## 执行要求

请按照 planner prompt 中的规则执行。

你需要输出：

- 一个完整 manifest JSON。
- full 模式下，所有讲义 `action` 为 `"new"`。
- incremental 模式下，根据 diff 判断每篇讲义的 `action`。

不要写任何 Markdown 讲义文件。

不要输出 JSON 以外的任何文字。

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content: ===README=== # FPGA Design Elements ## Copyright (c) 2019-2024 Charles Eric LaForest, PhD. A self-contained online book containing a library of FPGA design elements and related coding/design guides. You can read it online at https://fpgacpu.ca/fpga/ To obtain your own local copy: ``` git clone https://github.com/laforest/FPGADesignElements.git ``` then access [index.html](./index.html) from your favourite browser. All files are in one directory, so you can use it as a library in your CAD tools by simply importing all Verilog files. **IMPORTANT:** The module definitions are, by design, not usable as-is. Unless the design requires some minimum or constant value, all module parameters have a default value of 0 or an empty string. This is intentional, so when a user forgets to set a parameter when instantiating a module, synthesis will (almost always) fail, and linting also. Putting usable default values in the module definitions might not get noticed and cause bugs. This means the modules are not synthesizable as defined, but must be instantiated separately to set the parameters, which is what one normally does anyway. See [LICENSE](./LICENSE) for the details, but overall, you are free to use this book as you please. Contributions are welcome. Please email eric@fpgacpu.ca or Twitter @elaforest or join the Discord server. ===CONTRIBUTING=== # How to send in corrections/additions 1. Edit/Add the Verilog file (must have .v extension) * Please have a look at how other modules are written, and/or the [Verilog Coding Standard](./verilog.html) first. 2. Generate the HTML file by running the Verilog file through [v2h.py](./v2h.py) 3. Add a link in [index.html](./index.html) if necessary. 4. Submit your changes as a Pull Request ===.gitignore=== *.swp ===LICENSE=== MIT License Copyright (c) 2019-2024 Charles Eric LaForest, PhD. Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions: The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all copies or substantial portions of the Software. THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM, OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE SOFTWARE. ===tutorial dir=== total 28 drwxr-xr-x 3 runner runner 4096 Jul 8 11:21 . drwxr-xr-x 5 runner runner 20480 Jul 8 11:21 .. drwxr-xr-x 2 runner runner 4096 Jul 8 11:21 .transcripts ===tests dir=== total 32 drwxr-xr-x 2 runner runner 4096 Jul 8 11:21 . drwxr-xr-x 5 runner runner 20480 Jul 8 11:21 .. -rw-r--r-- 1 runner runner 1145 Jul 8 11:21 Counter_Gray_Tb.py -rw-r--r-- 1 runner runner 2674 Jul 8 11:21 Counter_Gray_Tb.sv

You will need the CSS Style file to render everything the same, but it's 51 in the repository and quite simple. 52 53
  • verilinter, which is a small script using Verilator and Icarus Verilog to lint Verilog-2001 56 files. This little tool catches a lot of simple and not so simple Verilog 57 mistakes and corner cases. 58 59
  • generate_file_skeleton.py, which 60 outputs a skeleton Verilog file in the style used in this book. Takes no 61 arguments. Simply pipe the output to a new file or your text editor window. 62 63
  • generate_instance.py, which generates 64 a quick-and-dirty module instance when given a filename containing a module 65 definition as a parameter. When code is highly modular, writing module 66 instances takes a large fraction of your writing time. This automates most of 67 the grunt work. Pipe the output to your text editor window. This is a quick 68 hack made to work only with the modules in this book. 69 70
  • generate_fusesoc_core_file, 71 which generates a local core file for 72 FuseSoC, the award-winning 73 package manager and set of build tools for HDL code. This allows you to 74 integrate the FPGA Design Elements into your FuseSoC projects. 75 76
  • Intel Quartus Prime. 77 The CAD tool suite for Intel (ex-Altera) FPGAs. It's smaller and easier to run 78 than Xilinx Vivado, and produces excellent RTL-level and post-synthesis 79 diagrams, which are very useful for checking and debugging designs. 80 81

FPGA Design Elements

Charles Eric LaForest, PhD.
GateForge Consulting, Ltd.

This is a continually evolving document. Please email any feedback to eric@fpgacpu.ca or Twitter @elaforest or join the Discord server.

Introduction

  • Outline
  • Licence and Disclaimer

References

  • Verilog Coding Standard
  • System Design Standard
  • Rules for Ready/Valid Handshakes and Synchronization

Tools

  • v2h.py, which converts commented Verilog files into HTML files. Line comments (no block comments!) are processed as Markdown. Thus, the web page doesn't get stale as the code evolves. You will need the CSS Style file to render everything the same, but it's in the repository and quite simple.
  • verilinter, which is a small script using Verilator and Icarus Verilog to lint Verilog-2001 files. This little tool catches a lot of simple and not so simple Verilog mistakes and corner cases.
  • generate_file_skeleton.py, which outputs a skeleton Verilog file in the style used in this book. Takes no arguments. Simply pipe the output to a new file or your text editor window.
  • generate_instance.py, which generates a quick-and-dirty module instance when given a filename containing a module definition as a parameter. When code is highly modular, writing module instances takes a large fraction of your writing time. This automates most of the grunt work. Pipe the output to your text editor window. This is a quick hack made to work only with the modules in this book.
  • generate_fusesoc_core_file, which generates a local core file for FuseSoC, the award-winning package manager and set of build tools for HDL code. This allows you to integrate the FPGA Design Elements into your FuseSoC projects.
  • Intel Quartus Prime. The CAD tool suite for Intel (ex-Altera) FPGAs. It's smaller and easier to run than Xilinx Vivado, and produces excellent RTL-level and post-synthesis diagrams, which are very useful for checking and debugging designs.

1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 Outline 9 10 11 12 13

Outline

14 15

This book provides a reference library of fundamental digital logic design 16 elements, along with standards for Verilog coding 17 and system design. Think of it as a hardware analog 18 to the C Standard Library ("libc") and its documentation.

19 20

The design elements are grouped into categories (e.g.: Boolean Logic, 21 Synchronous Logic, Integer Arithmetic, etc...). Each category entry implements 22 and explains a specific design element, starting with the most basic ones 23 (including ones meant solely for beginner's education rather than serious use), 24 and gradually increase in complexity up to complete interfaces and 25 special-purpose engines built using a common design form which heavily re-use 26 other design elements.

27 28
29 30

IMPORTANT: The module definitions are, by design, not usable as-is. 31 Unless the design requires some minimum or constant value, all module 32 parameters have a default value of 0 or an empty string. This is intentional, 33 so when a user forgets to set a parameter when instantiating a module, 34 synthesis will (almost always) fail, and linting also. Putting usable default 35 values in the module definitions might not get noticed and cause bugs. This 36 means the modules are not synthesizable as defined, but must be instantiated 37 separately to set the parameters, which is what one normally does anyway. 38 39

40 41

Each entry web page is generated directly from 42 the design element Verilog source, and you can find a link to the source at the 43 top of each page. Comments form the text body, while the code is placed in 44 bordered preformatted text blocks.

45 46

To obtain your own local copy of the book: git clone 47 https://github.com/laforest/FPGADesignElements.git then access ./index.html from your favourite browser. All Verilog 49 sources and other files are in one directory, so you can use it as a library in 50 your CAD tools by simply importing all Verilog files. See the license and disclaimer for the legal details, but 52 overall, you are free to use this book as you please.

53 54

Although the design elements in this book are written in Verilog-2001 (for 55 portability, better software support, and fine control of the synthesis), I 56 expect there is very little code which could not be trivially translated into 57 your Hardware Description Language of choice. I also expect the design elements 58 themselves to be universal and language-agnostic.

59 60

Contributions are welcome. Please email eric@fpgacpu.ca 61 or Twitter @elaforest or join the Discord server. 62 63

64

Back to FPGA Design Elements 65

file.content:
Outline

This book provides a reference library of fundamental digital logic design elements, along with standards for Verilog coding and system design. Think of it as a hardware analog to the C Standard Library ("libc") and its documentation.

The design elements are grouped into categories (e.g.: Boolean Logic, Synchronous Logic, Integer Arithmetic, etc...). Each category entry implements and explains a specific design element, starting with the most basic ones (including ones meant solely for beginner's education rather than serious use), and gradually increase in complexity up to complete interfaces and special-purpose engines built using a common design form which heavily re-use other design elements.

IMPORTANT: The module definitions are, by design, not usable as-is. Unless the design requires some minimum or constant value, all module parameters have a default value of 0 or an empty string. This is intentional, so when a user forgets to set a parameter when instantiating a module, synthesis will (almost always) fail, and linting also. Putting usable default values in the module definitions might not get noticed and cause bugs. This means the modules are not synthesizable as defined, but must be instantiated separately to set the parameters, which is what one normally does anyway.

Each entry web page is generated directly from the design element Verilog source, and you can find a link to the source at the top of each page. Comments form the text body, while the code is placed in bordered preformatted text blocks.

To obtain your own local copy of the book: git clone https://github.com/laforest/FPGADesignElements.git then access ./index.html from your favourite browser. All Verilog sources and other files are in one directory, so you can use it as a library in your CAD tools by simply importing all Verilog files. See the license and disclaimer for the legal details, but overall, you are free to use this book as you please.

Although the design elements in this book are written in Verilog-2001 (for portability, better software support, and fine control of the synthesis), I expect there is very little code which could not be trivially translated into your Hardware Description Language of choice. I also expect the design elements themselves to be universal and language-agnostic.

Contributions are welcome. Please email eric@fpgacpu.ca or Twitter @elaforest or join the Discord server.

1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 Rules for Ready/Valid Handshakes 9 10 11 12

Rules for Ready/Valid Handshakes and Synchronization

13 14

from FPGA Design Elements by Charles Eric LaForest, PhD. 15 16

Ready/valid handshakes are a flexible and lightweight way to connect and 17 control modules in composable ways, but as I designed more complex modules, I 18 found some corner cases I couldn't quite fit into the ready/valid handshake 19 model, and some designs started not composing well because I was implementing 20 the handshakes inconsistently. So I worked out a set of rules. I also found new 21 ways to distribute control logic, and I outline the synchronization process that 22 underlies ready/valid handshakes. 23 24

AXI is not Fundamental

25 26

I originally based these rules on those written in the AMBA AXI4 27 Specification, Chapter A3, "Single Interface Requirements", and 28 expanded on their meaning and consequences. I have replaced the inappropriate 29 and inaccurate "master/slave" terminology with "source/destination", 30 correspondingly, when refering to handshake interfaces. 31 32

Ready/valid handshakes are the underlying mechanism for AXI interfaces. Each 33 channel of AXI4 is a ready/valid interface, and the TLAST of AXI4-Stream is 34 merely a bit of metadata controlled by the ready/valid handshake. AXI4 and 35 AXI4-Stream interfaces bring in too many assumptions and complications for most 36 designs. It is simpler, smaller, and more flexible to compose your own 37 handshaking interfaces up from ready/valid primitives such as Skid Buffers and Arbiters and Forks and Joins, to only name a few. 42 43

Interfaces

44 45

There are two complementary interface types: source and 46 destination. Both interfaces have three signals: valid, ready, and 47 data. Ready and valid are single-bit signals, and the data signal is of 48 arbitrary width. All signals are synchronous to the rising edge of the 49 clock. 50 51

    52
  • The source interface outputs valid and data, and takes ready as input. 53
  • The destination interface outputs ready, and receives valid and data. 54
55 56

A connection always goes from the source interface to the destination 57 interface. Other pairings cannot work. 58 59

Loops

60 61

There must be no combinational paths from input to output signals in the 62 source interface (ready to valid), nor in the destination interface (valid to 63 ready). Otherwise combinational loops will form when connecting interfaces. 64 65

Even if only one type of interface has a loop, thus avoiding combinational 66 loops, the remaining combinational path will go from one interface to the other 67 and back to the first, which will cause a longer delay. This delay may both 68 limit your design clock frequency and make placement and routing of the modules 69 connected by these interfaces more difficult. It is not worth saving a 70 cycle of latency here at the expense of the performance of the rest of the 71 design. Proper handshake interface design and pipelining will avoid this 72 extra cycle of latency, while improving clock frequency and P&R. 73 74

ADDENDUM: Although the above points stand in theory, in practice 75 allowing a combinational path between valid and ready within an interface can 76 be a good tradeoff. Buffering every single source/destination connection can 77 bloat a design and will not improve performance except at the highest speeds. 78 Some pipeline control is most easily implemented combinationally between 79 interfaces, without buffering. However, you must then be careful of 80 combinational loops and of the requirement to avoid livelocks and deadlocks 81 (see below). 82 83

Handshake Procedure

84 85

At any time, the source interface raises and holds steady valid 86 when data is available, and the destination interface raises ready only when it 87 can accept more data. When both valid and ready are high, the handshake is 88 complete, the destination interface accepts the data in the same cycle, and the 89 ready and valid outputs change state if necessary. 90 91

The destination interface can freely assert and deassert ready at any time. 92 However, it is beneficial to have the destination interface assert ready as 93 soon as it can accept data, before the source interface asserts valid, to 94 shorten handshakes to a single cycle. For the same reason, the source interface 95 should assert and hold steady valid as soon as it has data to send. 96 97

After a handshake completes, the source interface drops valid if it has no 98 more data, otherwise it keeps valid high for the next handshake, which may 99 complete in the same clock cycle. Similarly, the destination interface drops 100 ready if it cannot accept more data, otherwise it keeps ready high and can 101 complete the next handshake in the same clock cycle if valid is also high. 102 103

Delayed Handshakes

104 105

It is possible to delay a handshake and have the destination interface 106 accept data at the moment the source interface asserts valid, then signal ready 107 to complete the handshake, after the accepted data is processed, to move the 108 source interface to the next data item. 109 110

Although this delayed handshake is legal and will work, it interferes with 111 conventional dataflow pipelining since the source interface cannot begin 112 processing and presenting the next data item concurrently with the destination 113 interface processing the current data item. If the source and destination 114 processing times are equal, which is the optimum when pipelining, this 115 incorrect handshake will halve the throughput instead of doubling it! 116 117

However, when full throughput pipelining is not needed or possible (e.g.: an 118 iterative calculation), a delayed handshake can implement a very useful control 119 mechanism by signalling to the source interface when the next item can be 120 processed. A Half Buffer implements 121 this control mechanism by accepting an item from the source interface, and 122 internally presenting that item and a valid signal at its own source interface. 123 Detecting the rise of valid with a Pulse 124 Generator starts the internal logic, whose control logic now only need to 125 pulse ready when the calculation is done to complete the handshake. This 126 simplifies control and maintains concurrency. 127 128

Sampling Changing Data

129 130

Typically, the source interface holds data steady alongside valid, changing 131 data only after the handshake completes. Any data value outside of the clock 132 cycle when the handshake completes is lost. It is allowable to have data be a 133 continuously changing value, synchronously to the clock, which will get sampled 134 whenever a handshake completes. 135 136

Sampling changing data is best done by having the source interface hold 137 valid high, while the destination interface asserts ready at an interval. The 138 opposite method of sampling where the source interface periodically asserts 139 valid does not guarantee a predictable sampling interval, or cause a livelock, 140 since it depends on the destination interface having already asserted ready. It 141 may also cause a deadlock if the destination interface waits for valid before 142 raising ready (see below). 143 144

Avoiding Deadlocks and Livelocks

145 146

We must constrain the behaviour of the valid and ready signals to prevent 147 deadlocks, where the source and destination interfaces wait forever for 148 eachother to respond, and to prevent livelocks, where both interfaces are 149 responding but the handshake never completes. 150 151

To prevent deadlocks, the source interface must not wait until the 152 destination interface asserts ready before asserting valid, while the 153 destination interface can wait for the source interface to assert valid before 154 asserting ready. This waiting will extend the handshake to two cycles, 155 completing in the second cycle, but has applications where we want to 156 selectively complete handshakes from multiple source interfaces, as in an Arbiter. 158 159

To prevent livelocks, when the source interface asserts valid it must 160 remain asserted until the handshake completes, else we could end up in a 161 situation where the source interface temporarily asserts valid and the 162 destination interface temporarily asserts ready, but they never coincide to 163 complete the handshake. 164 165

Reset

166 167

The reset signal to modules with source and/or destination interfaces can be 168 active high or low, may assert asynchronously, but must deassert synchronously. 169 However, using an active high reset limits the amount of inverted logic to 170 read, which keeps the logic clearer. Also, I strongly recommend using a 171 fully synchronous reset since an asynchronous reset will inhibit register 172 retiming. Any latch holding state inside the source or destination 173 interface must be reset, else the interface may remain in the wrong state after 174 reset (e.g. the source interface signalling that data is valid when there isn't 175 any). 176 177

Internal State

178 179

Any internal state of a module which affects a source or destination 180 interface must only ever change in the same cycle as a completed 181 handshake, when both ready and valid are raised together. Otherwise, data may 182 be lost. 183 184

For example, if you are counting down words in a packet passing through a 185 source interface, and you change state as soon as the counter reaches zero, you 186 will lose the last packet word if, by coincidence, the ready signal of the 187 destination interface goes low at the same time as the counter reaches zero. By 188 the time the destination interface raises ready, the source interface has 189 already moved on to the next packet and the previous data word has been lost, 190 corrupting the previous packet and all following packets. 191 192

Thus, as a rule, define a line of code like this one: 193 194 

195	    always @(*) begin
196	        handshake_complete = (ready == 1'b1) && (valid == 1'b1);
197	    end
198
199 200 and use it to gate any state transitions that affect a source or destination 201 interface. 202 203

Underlying Synchronization

204 205

Since we very usefully partition the design of computating machinery as data 206 and control paths, it's natural to think of one module as transferring data to 207 another, and of one module as controlling the operation of another. The 208 ready/valid handshake is a great fit to both data transfers and module control. 209 210

However, ready/valid handshakes are only a particular implementation of a 211 more fundamental mechanism of synchronization (a.k.a.: 212 rendez-vous), upon which we then build up any combination of data 213 transfer and control we need. This is another reason "master/slave" terminology 214 (and its analogs) should be discarded: it assumes constraints to the design 215 process which are not fundamental or always necessary. These constraints may be 216 useful in that they help us manage the complexity of a design, but at the cost 217 of an increased mismatch between the desired computation and its 218 implementation, which increases complexity and testing difficulty relative to a 219 more closely matched design. 220 221

To illustrate this underlying synchronization mechanism, imagine two 222 modules which operate concurrently, but must sometimes synchronize with 223 eachother, for any number of possible reasons (e.g.: minimizing buffering, 224 preventing pipeline stalls, keeping audio and video streams synchronized). 225 Each module has an input named "OK_IN", and an output named "OK_OUT", and we 226 simply connect these outputs to those inputs as expected. 227 228

When one module needs to synchronize, it raises and holds high "OK_OUT" and 229 waits. Eventually, the other module will do the same and both modules will 230 simultaneously see (OK_IN == 1'b1) && (OK_OUT == 1'b1) == 1'b1 231 during the same clock cycle, and their control logics can change state also 232 simultaneously, if not identically. The modules have synchronized, no data was 233 transferred, and neither one is in control of the other. 234 235

(This is similar, but not identical to passing both module outputs through a 236 Pipeline Synchronizer, as that 237 includes the downstream module(s), implementing a 3-way synchronization and 238 data transfer.) 239 240

If we then use this synchronization mechanism to transfer data in one 241 direction, we have re-invented ready/valid interfaces. We could also implement 242 a bidirectional simultaneous data transfer the same way, with the understanding 243 that both "OK" signals must follow the same deadlock and livelock avoidance 244 rules mentioned before. 245 246

Finally, some readers will notice the similarity between this 247 synchronization mechanism and the 2-phase and 4-phase handshakes used in 248 asynchronous logic, only simplified since we have a clock as an absolute time 249 reference. 250 251

Back to FPGA Design Elements 252 253 254 255

Rules for Ready/Valid Handshakes and Synchronization

from FPGA Design Elements by Charles Eric LaForest, PhD.

Ready/valid handshakes are a flexible and lightweight way to connect and control modules in composable ways, but as I designed more complex modules, I found some corner cases I couldn't quite fit into the ready/valid handshake model, and some designs started not composing well because I was implementing the handshakes inconsistently. Each channel of AXI4 is a ready/valid interface, and the TLAST of AXI4-Stream is merely a bit of metadata controlled by the ready/valid handshake.   • The destination interface outputs ready, and receives valid and data.

A connection always goes from the source interface to the destination interface. Other pairings cannot work.

At any time, the source interface raises and holds steady valid when data is available, and the destination interface raises ready only when it can accept more data. When both valid and ready are high, the handshake is complete, the destination interface accepts the data in the same cycle, and the ready and valid outputs change state if necessary.

The destination interface can freely assert and deassert ready at any time. However, it is beneficial to have the destination interface assert ready as soon as it can accept data, before the source interface asserts valid, to shorten handshakes to a single cycle. For the same reason, the source interface should assert and hold steady valid as soon as it has data to send.

After a handshake completes, the source interface drops valid if it has no more data, otherwise it keeps valid high for the next handshake, which may complete in the same clock cycle. Similarly, the destination interface drops ready if it cannot accept more data, otherwise it keeps ready high and can complete the next handshake in the same clock cycle if valid is also high.

It is possible to delay a handshake and have the destination interface accept data at the moment the source interface asserts valid, then signal ready to complete the handshake, after the accepted data is processed, to move the source interface to the next data item.

It is allowable to have data be a continuously changing value, synchronously to the clock, which will get sampled whenever a handshake completes.

The opposite method of sampling where the source interface periodically asserts valid does not guarantee a predictable sampling interval, or cause a livelock, since it depends on the destination interface having already asserted ready. This waiting will extend the handshake to two cycles, completing in the second cycle, but has applications where we want to selectively complete handshakes from multiple source interfaces, as in an Arbiter.

To prevent livelocks, when the source interface asserts valid it must remain asserted until the handshake completes, else we could end up in a situation where the source interface temporarily asserts valid and the destination interface temporarily asserts ready, but they never coincide to complete the handshake.

    always @(*) begin

and use it to gate any state transitions that affect a source or destination interface.

Underlying Synchronization

Since we very usefully partition the design of computating machinery as data and control paths, it's natural to think of one module as transferring data to another, and of one module as controlling the operation of another. However, ready/valid handshakes are only a particular implementation of a more fundamental mechanism of synchronization (a.k.a.: rendez-vous), upon which we then build up any combination of data transfer and control we need. These constraints may be useful in that they help us manage the complexity of a design, but at the cost of an increased mismatch between the desired computation and its implementation, which increases complexity and testing difficulty relative to a more closely matched design.

To illustrate this underlying synchronization mechanism, imagine two modules which operate concurrently, but must sometimes synchronize with eachother, for any number of possible reasons (e.g.: minimizing buffering, preventing pipeline stalls, keeping audio and video streams synchronized). Each module has an input named "OK_IN", and an output named "OK_OUT", and we simply connect these outputs to those inputs as expected.

When one module needs to synchronize, it raises and holds high "OK_OUT" and waits. The modules have synchronized, no data was transferred, and neither one is in control of the other.

(This is similar, but not identical to passing both module outputs through a Pipeline Synchronizer, as that includes the downstream module(s), implementing a 3-way synchronization and data transfer.)

If we then use this synchronization mechanism to transfer data in one direction, we have re-invented ready/valid interfaces. content.content:
1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 System Design Standard 9 10 11 12

System Design Standard

13 14

from FPGA Design Elements by Charles Eric LaForest, PhD. 15 16

This is a continually evolving document. Please send any 17 feedback to eric@fpgacpu.ca or @elaforest or join the Discord server. 19 20

Over time, I've found that most of the difficulty in FPGA system design 21 comes from a lack of modularity, not having a library of building blocks, and 22 from not using the logic optimization work done by the CAD tool to simplify 23 design. 24 25

It matters to have a system design standard, since the implementation 26 doesn't matter. We want to think in terms of the design and its 27 blocks, and not concern ourselves (too much) with the implementation of these 28 blocks. Nonetheless, a block needs to be well-implemented and understood. I 29 describe related implementation practices in the companion Verilog Coding Standard. 31 32

Scope

33 34

This standard mainly describes how to divide a digital design into parts, 35 including all the files required by the CAD tools which do not describe the 36 design itself. These parts aim to be reusable, easy to understand, and 37 cultivate a library of parts and design idioms. 38 39

This standard does not define any specification, testing, 40 verification, validation, or documentation methodologies (though I hope you 41 have some!). 42 43

Nor does this standard specify an implementation language, method or CAD 44 tool. However, it was written in tandem with a Verilog 45 Coding Standard. 46 47

Finally, this standard assumes you are already familiar with digital design 48 in general. It is not a language or tool reference. 49 50

Modularization

51 52 Divide your design into modules to a very fine degree, even to the point of a 53 simple vector of AND gates. 54 When a design is entirely composed of modules, the code expresses the design 55 itself rather than its implementation, and gives you multiple forms of 56 documentation and checking for free, and greater control over implementation. 57 58

A module definition forms part of a general design intent and a 59 module instance, through its name, parameters, and connections, tells you the 60 specific design intent at that point in the design. Without extensive 61 modularization, unless the reader already knows the design, they have to 62 continually reverse-engineer the meaning of the logic back into the design. 63 64

A design composed of modules, when successively viewed in the CAD tool as 65 elaboration, synthesis, and implementation schematics, allows you to check many 66 things: 67 68

    69 70
  • The post-elaboration schematic gives you a block diagram view of your 71 source code, which allows you to more easily find missing connections and count 72 pipeline stages (and thus check their alignment). 73 74
  • The post-synthesis schematic allows you to check that your code synthesizes 75 as you expect, and shows you the impact of logic optimizations: hardware will 76 disappear from a module or move from one module to another. If an entire module 77 vanishes, that may indicate a missing connection to that module which disabled 78 it. 79 80
  • The post-place-and-route schematic (a.k.a. post-implementation) shows you 81 how the synthesized logic ends up on the FPGA, and is often annotated with 82 logic and interconnect delays. This view can also show you the critical path as 83 it flows from module to module, and that knowledge can feed back into the 84 architecture of your design. 85 86
87 88

Without modules, the schematics rapidly become a large mess of random logic, 89 which becomes increasingly hard to relate to the original code as you move from 90 elaboration to sythesis to implementation. Dividing a design into modules 91 preserves the logical design hierarchy (even though in hardware it is usually 92 all flattened). 93 94

Within each module, you only have to think about the 95 immediately relevant logic and wires, which give us the guiding principle for 96 modular decomposition of a design: move unrelated connections into separate 97 modules. If you have lines of logic with connections between them which 98 themselves do not connect to the surrounding logic, then those lines should be, 99 if possible, parameterized and encapsulated into a sub-module. Then, the other 100 connections in the surrounding logic are necessarily more closely related and 101 now encapsulated in the enclosing module. 102 103

Building Blocks

104 105 Use the modularization process to create a library of well-commented and tested 106 building blocks which will largely remove the need for random logic in most 107 cases, and document design knowledge for the future. 108 109

By adjusting the module parameters, setting some inputs constant, and 110 leaving some outputs unconnected, the CAD tool can optimize the general case 111 logic of a building block, while still keeping the design intent clear in the 112 source. A design then becomes a hierarchy of modules, connected by wires, and 113 any random logic signifies a very local and special case (e.g.: combining two 114 flags into one). 115 116

These building blocks should be quite small, implementing logic functions 117 common to most any design: FPGA Design Elements. 118 119

Using building blocks enables a better separation of control, processing, 120 and interface in your design. Divide a sub-system into one module for 121 functionality (processing and storage), one for control (the FSM), and one for 122 interfacing to the rest of the system (memory-mapping the data storage and 123 configuration registers). 124 125

System Architecture

126 127

In a project with multiple FPGAs and boards and connecting paths between 128 them, all designed by separate engineering teams, a common code architecture on 129 each FPGA makes managing various mismatches, errors, and last-minute changes 130 much easier, as well as improving portability. It also help single-FPGA designs 131 for the same reasons. 132 133

I'll start explaining at the bottom and work upwards. Each higher layer 134 instantiates the one below it as a single module, and performs one specific 135 function to support that underlying module. As we move upwards, the functions 136 shift from creating and supporting the application logic to managing the 137 interface to the outside world: 138 139

    140
  • The Core and Instance modules deal with the application logic. 141
  • The Adapter module interfaces the application logic to the specific FPGA device. 142
  • The Shim module interfaces the FPGA to the specific PCB it's mounted on. 143
144 145

Core

146 147

The Core logic of the application contains all the parts needed in the 148 abstract, without concern for interface protocols, size of FPGA, number of 149 instances, etc... It may culminate in a single module or a few. The only 150 FPGA-specific parts are technology-mapping issues like which DSP or BRAM blocks 151 are used. Other than porting to another FPGA family, this code should never 152 change. For example, the core logic could contain the control and data paths 153 of a soft-CPU, and the building blocks for a network-on-chip, but all as a 154 library of parts. 155 156

Instance

157 158

The Instance culminates in a single module which instantiates the Core 159 logic, and specifies the size and number of core logic modules, as well as all 160 the wiring between them. The Instance primarily deals with scaling the 161 Core logic to the specific FPGA device being used. For example, the instance 162 defines how many data paths a soft-CPU will have if it's a SIMD system, and how 163 many CPUs in total and the network-on-chip which connects them. There are still 164 no concerns about particular external interfaces: buses and clocks are simple 165 signals. 166 167

At this point, the design can be simulated to check for logical correctness, 168 and synthesized for a preliminary estimate of its final operating frequency, 169 assuming the instance is surrounded by a temporary test harness of registers to 170 ensure correct static timing analysis. 171 172 173 174

Adapter

175 176

The Adapter instantiates the Instance, defines the final interface ports 177 expected by the board upon which the FPGA resides, and wires up the instance to 178 those ports. The Adapter adds any logic necessary to support the Instance and 179 also satisfy the FPGA CAD tools. For example, the Adapter will contain any 180 clock generation and management hardware, add any differential buffers to 181 signals that need it, even if unused, to satisfy CAD tool constraints, and 182 convert interface protocols as required (e.g.: as AXI leader/followers, 183 bi-directional or tri-state buffers, etc...). 184 185

At this point, synthesis of the design should find any final errors, such as 186 insufficient clock buffers or logic resources, expose any missing/violated 187 constraints, and in general point out anything that needs attention. You 188 should, of course, fix things to minimize the number of warnings from the CAD 189 tool. 190 191

Shim

192 193

The Shim instantiates the Adapter, and nothing else. It has the same 194 interface ports as the Adapter, and the Shim's ports are at the top level and 195 physically connect to the FPGA pins. What the Shim does, however, is handle the 196 problems that crop up with PCBs: unusable connections, schematic errors, 197 incompatible voltage standards, etc... For example, if some top-level pins are 198 unusable due to a design or manufacturing error, the Shim can shuffle the 199 signals between the top-level pins and the adapter ports so as to present a 200 uniform, maybe reduced, interface to the Adapter. If you have spare board pins, 201 this is where you use them. 202 203

Another example is where, due to how a PCB had been designed, the pins of a 204 bus were not connected to consecutive pins along the FPGA's I/O Banks, 205 scattering the bus (and it's associated logic) all over the device. The Shim 206 file translates the logical bus pins from the adapter to physically consecutive 207 pins on the device, which helped keep related logic grouped together. Of 208 course, any other FPGA on the PCB needs a similar Shim file to re-organize the 209 now scrambled bus signals coming in. 210 211

The Shim file is also a good place to annotate the top level pins: which 212 ones are clock-capable, are they usable as differential pairs, which clock 213 region or I/O Bank they reside in, etc... which helps future alterations. 214 215

Finally, you now can do a full synthesis to get the final operating 216 frequency, do post-placement synthesis to check things like the I/O delay 217 lines, and do final floorplanning. 218 219

Project Constraints

220 221

Divide different types of CAD tool constraints across different files, 222 leaving only a select few constraints applied directly in the source as 223 attributes on wires, registers, and modules. This keeps the design as portable 224 as possible and easy to manage when the underlying FPGA or the surrounding PCB 225 changes. Some constraint types to group into separate files include: 226 227

    228 229
  • Bitstream and configuration, where you define pull-ups/downs on 230 configuration pins, what configuration interface is used (e.g.: SPI, parallel 231 port, JTAG, etc...), and bistream encryption. 232 233
  • Logic placement, where you define floorplan regions and assign modules to 234 them. This is handy to isolate critical logic that needs to meet timing 235 reliably as the design grows (e.g.: DDR controllers), and to work around some 236 critical paths by physically separating modules which otherwise get placed too 237 close together and cause congested routing. 238 239
  • Pin placement, where for each bank of device pins you give each pin a source code 240 name, an I/O standard, pull-ups/down, drive strengths, etc.. and make note of 241 which pins remain unused, or are particularly dangerous to the design if 242 misused or misconfigured. Grouping the pins by bank in device order also shows 243 you which signals physically adjacent to eachother on the device, which matters 244 for noise and switching current issues. 245 246
  • Timing constraints, where you define the incoming clocks, their 247 synchronous/asynchronous relationships, any false paths, and external I/O 248 delays. 249 250
  • Optimization constraints, which allow you to preserve logic against some 251 optimizations. For example, when you bring up a partially completed system 252 where some modules are dangling, or have incomplete connections, preventing 253 optimization on those modules (e.g.: via a DONT_TOUCH attribute 254 under Vivado) to keep any unused logic will give you a better ongoing estimate 255 of the final area and timing. 256 257
258 259

However, some of these constraints may have to be applied in the source code 260 itself, as the CAD tool may not read the other constraints files early enough 261 in the synthesis, placement, or routing phases. This really depends on the CAD 262 tool. And some constraints, like Vivado's ASYNC_REG which declares 263 registers as part of a clock domain crossing synchronizer, should be placed in 264 the CDC synchronizer module source code so they automatically take effect at 265 each CDC synchronizer module instantiation. 266 267

268 269 fpgacpu.ca 270

System Design Standard

from FPGA Design Elements by Charles Eric LaForest, PhD.

This is a continually evolving document. Please send any feedback to eric@fpgacpu.ca or @elaforest or join the Discord server.

Over time, I've found that most of the difficulty in FPGA system design comes from a lack of modularity, not having a library of building blocks, and from not using the logic optimization work done by the CAD tool to simplify design.

It matters to have a system design standard, since the implementation doesn't matter. We want to think in terms of the design and its blocks, and not concern ourselves (too much) with the implementation of these blocks. Nonetheless, a block needs to be well-implemented and understood. I describe related implementation practices in the companion Verilog Coding Standard.

Scope

This standard mainly describes how to divide a digital design into parts, including all the files required by the CAD tools which do not describe the design itself. These parts aim to be reusable, easy to understand, and cultivate a library of parts and design idioms.

This standard does not define any specification, testing, verification, validation, or documentation methodologies (though I hope you have some!).

Nor does this standard specify an implementation language, method or CAD tool. However, it was written in tandem with a Verilog Coding Standard.

Finally, this standard assumes you are already familiar with digital design in general. It is not a language or tool reference.

Modularization

Divide your design into modules to a very fine degree, even to the point of a simple vector of AND gates. When a design is entirely composed of modules, the code expresses the design itself rather than its implementation, and gives you multiple forms of documentation and checking for free, and greater control over implementation.

A module definition forms part of a general design intent and a module instance, through its name, parameters, and connections, tells you the specific design intent at that point in the design. Without extensive modularization, unless the reader already knows the design, they have to continually reverse-engineer the meaning of the logic back into the design.

A design composed of modules, when successively viewed in the CAD tool as elaboration, synthesis, and implementation schematics, allows you to check many things:

  • The post-elaboration schematic gives you a block diagram view of your source code, which allows you to more easily find missing connections and count pipeline stages (and thus check their alignment).
  • The post-synthesis schematic allows you to check that your code synthesizes as you expect, and shows you the impact of logic optimizations: hardware will disappear from a module or move from one module to another. If an entire module vanishes, that may indicate a missing connection to that module which disabled it.
  • The post-place-and-route schematic (a.k.a. post-implementation) shows you how the synthesized logic ends up on the FPGA, and is often annotated with logic and interconnect delays. This view can also show you the critical path as it flows from module to module, and that knowledge can feed back into the architecture of your design.

Without modules, the schematics rapidly become a large mess of random logic, which becomes increasingly hard to relate to the original code as you move from elaboration to sythesis to implementation. Dividing a design into modules preserves the logical design hierarchy (even though in hardware it is usually all flattened).

Within each module, you only have to think about the immediately relevant logic and wires, which give us the guiding principle for modular decomposition of a design: move unrelated connections into separate modules. If you have lines of logic with connections between them which themselves do not connect to the surrounding logic, then those lines should be, if possible, parameterized and encapsulated into a sub-module. Then, the other connections in the surrounding logic are necessarily more closely related and now encapsulated in the enclosing module.

Building Blocks

Use the modularization process to create a library of well-commented and tested building blocks which will largely remove the need for random logic in most cases, and document design knowledge for the future.

By adjusting the module parameters, setting some inputs constant, and leaving some outputs unconnected, the CAD tool can optimize the general case logic of a building block, while still keeping the design intent clear in the source. A design then becomes a hierarchy of modules, connected by wires, and any random logic signifies a very local and special case (e.g.: combining two flags into one).

These building blocks should be quite small, implementing logic functions common to most any design: FPGA Design Elements.

Using building blocks enables a better separation of control, processing, and interface in your design. Divide a sub-system into one module for functionality (processing and storage), one for control (the FSM), and one for interfacing to the rest of the system (memory-mapping the data storage and configuration registers).

System Architecture

In a project with multiple FPGAs and boards and connecting paths between them, all designed by separate engineering teams, a common code architecture on each FPGA makes managing various mismatches, errors, and last-minute changes much easier, as well as improving portability. It also help single-FPGA designs for the same reasons.

I'll start explaining at the bottom and work upwards. Each higher layer instantiates the one below it as a single module, and performs one specific function to support that underlying module. As we move upwards, the functions shift from creating and supporting the application logic to managing the interface to the outside world:

  • The Core and Instance modules deal with the application logic.
  • The Adapter module interfaces the application logic to the specific FPGA device.
  • The Shim module interfaces the FPGA to the specific PCB it's mounted on.

Core

The Core logic of the application contains all the parts needed in the abstract, without concern for interface protocols, size of FPGA, number of instances, etc... It may culminate in a single module or a few. The only FPGA-specific parts are technology-mapping issues like which DSP or BRAM blocks are used. Other than porting to another FPGA family, this code should never change. For example, the core logic could contain the control and data paths of a soft-CPU, and the building blocks for a network-on-chip, but all as a library of parts.

Instance

The Instance culminates in a single module which instantiates the Core logic, and specifies the size and number of core logic modules, as well as all the wiring between them. The Instance primarily deals with scaling the Core logic to the specific FPGA device being used. For example, the instance defines how many data paths a soft-CPU will have if it's a SIMD system, and how many CPUs in total and the network-on-chip which connects them. There are still no concerns about particular external interfaces: buses and clocks are simple signals.

At this point, the design can be simulated to check for logical correctness, and synthesized for a preliminary estimate of its final operating frequency, assuming the instance is surrounded by a temporary test harness of registers to ensure correct static timing analysis.

Adapter

The Adapter instantiates the Instance, defines the final interface ports expected by the board upon which the FPGA resides, and wires up the instance to those ports. The Adapter adds any logic necessary to support the Instance and also satisfy the FPGA CAD tools. For example, the Adapter will contain any clock generation and management hardware, add any differential buffers to signals that need it, even if unused, to satisfy CAD tool constraints, and convert interface protocols as required (e.g.: as AXI leader/followers, bi-directional or tri-state buffers, etc...).

At this point, synthesis of the design should find any final errors, such as insufficient clock buffers or logic resources, expose any missing/violated constraints, and in general point out anything that needs attention. You should, of course, fix things to minimize the number of warnings from the CAD tool.

Shim

The Shim instantiates the Adapter, and nothing else. It has the same interface ports as the Adapter, and the Shim's ports are at the top level and physically connect to the FPGA pins. What the Shim does, however, is handle the problems that crop up with PCBs: unusable connections, schematic errors, incompatible voltage standards, etc... For example, if some top-level pins are unusable due to a design or manufacturing error, the Shim can shuffle the signals between the top-level pins and the adapter ports so as to present a uniform, maybe reduced, interface to the Adapter. If you have spare board pins, this is where you use them.

Another example is where, due to how a PCB had been designed, the pins of a bus were not connected to consecutive pins along the FPGA's I/O Banks, scattering the bus (and it's associated logic) all over the device. The Shim file translates the logical bus pins from the adapter to physically consecutive pins on the device, which helped keep related logic grouped together. Of course, any other FPGA on the PCB needs a similar Shim file to re-organize the now scrambled bus signals coming in.

The Shim file is also a good place to annotate the top level pins: which ones are clock-capable, are they usable as differential pairs, which clock region or I/O Bank they reside in, etc... which helps future alterations.

Finally, you now can do a full synthesis to get the final operating frequency, do post-placement synthesis to check things like the I/O delay lines, and do final floorplanning.

Project Constraints

Divide different types of CAD tool constraints across different files, leaving only a select few constraints applied directly in the source as attributes on wires, registers, and modules. This keeps the design as portable as possible and easy to manage when the underlying FPGA or the surrounding PCB changes. Some constraint types to group into separate files include:

  • Bitstream and configuration, where you define pull-ups/downs on configuration pins, what configuration interface is used (e.g.: SPI, parallel port, JTAG, etc...), and bistream encryption.
  • Logic placement, where you define floorplan regions and assign modules to them. This is handy to isolate critical logic that needs to meet timing reliably as the design grows (e.g.: DDR controllers), and to work around some critical paths by physically separating modules which otherwise get placed too close together and cause congested routing.
  • Pin placement, where for each bank of device pins you give each pin a source code name, an I/O standard, pull-ups/down, drive strengths, etc.. and make note of which pins remain unused, or are particularly dangerous to the design if misused or misconfigured. Grouping the pins by bank in device order also shows you which signals physically adjacent to eachother on the device, which matters for noise and switching current issues.
  • Timing constraints, where you define the incoming clocks, their synchronous/asynchronous relationships, any false paths, and external I/O delays.
  • Optimization constraints, which allow you to preserve logic against some optimizations. For example, when you bring up a partially completed system where some modules are dangling, or have incomplete connections, preventing optimization on those modules (e.g.: via a DONT_TOUCH attribute under Vivado) to keep any unused logic will give you a better ongoing estimate of the final area and timing.

However, some of these constraints may have to be applied in the source code itself, as the CAD tool may not read the other constraints files early enough in the synthesis, placement, or routing phases. This really depends on the CAD tool. And some constraints, like Vivado's ASYNC_REG which declares registers as part of a clock domain crossing synchronizer, should be placed in the CDC synchronizer module source code so they automatically take effect at each CDC synchronizer module instantiation.

fpgacpu.ca

content.content:
1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 Verilog Coding Standard 9 10 11 12

Verilog Coding Standard

13 14

from FPGA Design Elements by Charles Eric LaForest, PhD. 15 16

This is a continually evolving document. Please send any 17 feedback to eric@fpgacpu.ca or @elaforest or join the Discord server. 19 20

Over time, I've found that most of the difficulty with Verilog is a problem 21 with programming practice which goes away with a certain coding style, which I 22 describe here. It's not perfect, but it works and makes Verilog programming 23 easier, more pleasant, and more reliable. 24 25

It matters to have a Verilog coding standard, because Verilog doesn't 26 matter. We want to quickly get past the nuts-and-bolts of Verilog itself 27 so we can focus on the system we are trying to build. I describe the related 28 system design practice in the companion System Design 29 Standard. 30 31

Scope

32 33

This standard was written with FPGAs in mind so some details may not apply, 34 or may be missing, in relation to ASICs. Nonetheless, the standard should still 35 largely be applicable to ASIC design. 36 37

This standard mainly defines a restricted form of Verilog which uses a 38 limited number of programming idioms. These restrictions reduce bugs, make the 39 code easier to understand, and aim to give consistent synthesis results across 40 CAD tools, whether the code is manually written or machine-generated. 41 42

This standard does not define any typesetting conventions such as 43 brace style, indentation, variable naming, comment format, etc... You will see 44 code examples typeset in a style I recommend, but it is not essential. Like 45 regular written language prose, anything that helps readability is good. 46 47

Nor does this standard define any testing, simulation, verification, or 48 validation methodologies (though I hope you have some!). However, it will 49 address some pitfalls and low-level details. 50 51

Finally, this standard assumes you are already familiar with Verilog and 52 digital design in general. It is not a language or tool reference. 53 54

Verilog Language Versions

55 56

Use Verilog-2001, specifically its synthesizable subset, as it's well 57 supported across CAD tools. Anything not synthesizable is for 58 simulation/verification and outside the scope of this standard. 59 60

The previous version, Verilog-1995, lacks several useful features (named 61 port connections, vector part select, generate blocks, etc...) without which 62 your code will get longer and more difficult. Avoid mixing Verilog-1995 forms 63 into Verilog-2001 code, despite the backwards compatibility. 64 65

The following version, SystemVerilog, brings in too many features and thus 66 has uneven support across CAD tools. It does have desirable features 67 (interfaces, parameterizable port instances, structs, enums, etc...), but until 68 support matures and a similarly restricted, synthesizable subset can be agreed 69 upon, it should be avoided or used for simulation/verification (which it is 70 well suited for!). 71 72

Definitions and Inclusions

73 74

Use `include directives only to bring in common function 75 definitions which, due to a limitation of Verilog-2001, must be defined inside 76 the body of a module. A common example is an integer logarithm function to 77 calculate address width. Place the function in a separate file and include it 78 at the start of the body of modules which need it. 79 80

Use `define directives only for values which remain valid and 81 constant everywhere and always in the design, don't depend on any 82 other less-constant values, and can't be passed as module parameters (e.g.: 83 opcode encodings for ALU functional units). Place these definitions into a 84 separate file and `include it at the beginning of files which need 85 them. Otherwise, construct constants within a module using 86 localparam. 87 88

Basic Types and Values

89 90

All variables, modules, constants, etc... must be defined before they are 91 used in a file, going from top to bottom. If you have a wire connecting the 92 output of a later module to the input of an earlier module, define the wire 93 first, before the earliest module using it. 94 95

Defaults

96 97

Use `default_nettype none at the start of each file, before 98 module definition. This causes any undefined variable to be an error. 99 Otherwise, accidentally undefined variables become 1-bit wires, which may cause 100 subtle bugs during synthesis. 101 102

For code you cannot control which assumes a default net type (e.g. 103 vendor-provided models and IP blocks), wrap the code as follows inside the 104 provided source file: 105 106 

107	`default_nettype wire
108	
109	...
110	
111	`default_nettype none
112
113 114

Initialization and Logic Values

115 116 117 118

Use only reg and wire types. All signals are 119 registers except where a wire is mandatory: module input ports, connecting 120 module ports together, and inferring tri-state I/O. (See Assign Statements for details.) 122 123

Only use 0,1,X,Z values, and avoid X and Z if at all possible. 124 Do not assign X to registers or wires, as X propagation will make testing 125 difficult, and isn't supported in 2-state simulators (e.g.: Verilator). 126 Instead, have a default catch-all or fall-through logic expression which takes 127 effect when all others don't. The same idea applies to Boolean tests. Z is only 128 used to define tri-state I/O, and only if the CAD vendor does not provide 129 tri-state I/O primitives, as the inference is unreliable. 130 131

All registers must be initialized (and reset, if necessary) with a value 132 which does not contain X or Z, either at definition or using an 133 initial block. Note that some combinations of register reset and 134 initialization may not work well together depending on the FPGA family. See Resets for details. 136 137

Never assign an initial value to wires, either using an explicit assign statement or an implicit initial assignment (e.g.: 139 wire foo = bar;). Assigning a wire an initial value can cause 140 problem if you then mistakenly connect that wire to a module port output, 141 leading to multiple drivers on the wire and surprise X's in your simulation. 142 This situation easily happens if you convert a reg to a 143 wire, because the signal source is now in a module, and you forget 144 to remove the initial assignment on the reg (usually a zero). 145 146

Parameterization

147 148

Wherever possible, parameterize the width of your variables rather than 149 using constants. It's more work initially, but makes modules more general and 150 avoids tedious and error-prone edits when something inevitably changes. If the 151 width can be set by the user, then use a module parameter. If it's an internal 152 module constant, use a localparam. Very, very rarely, use a `define constant. 153 Never use a hardcoded literal integer: name it with a localparam instead. 154 155

Unfortunately, in Verilog-2001, module parameters and localparams are not 156 valid width specifiers for literal values, only `define constants and literal 157 integers are. This makes initializing registers of parameterized width with 158 values of the exact same width impossible, and it's unclear if the implicit 159 width-extension for values like 'b0 or 'd1 work 160 correctly past 32 or 64 bits width, depending on the CAD tool. As a workaround, 161 use concatenation and replication to create the value you need of the proper 162 bit width. 163 164

Also note that localparam is not legal in the ports definition of a module 165 definition. That's only in SystemVerilog. Thus, if you need a constant 166 parameter for a port width or some other parameter calculation, you have to use 167 a parameter, and leave a comment that it should not be set at module 168 instantiation. 169 170 

171	// This is 32-bits by default
172	localparam WORD_WIDTH = 72;
173	
174	// Also legal to define bit width
175	localparam [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] WORD_CONSTANT = `SOME_DEFINED_CONSTANT;
176	
177	// Not legal
178	reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] foo = WORD_WIDTH'b0;
179	
180	// Legal workarounds
181	localparam WORD_ZERO      = {WORD_WIDTH{1'b0}};
182	localparam WORD_ONE       = {{WORD_WIDTH-1{1'b0}},1'b1};
183	localparam WORD_MINUS_ONE = ~WORD_ZERO;
184	
185	reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] foo  = WORD_ZERO;
186	
187	// Simpler, but unclear if extended with zeros or Xs past 32 or 64 bits
188	reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] foo = 'b0;
189	reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] foo = 'd42;
190	
191	// Sometimes you have to clip at the point of use
192	localparam NARROW_ZERO = {NARROW_WIDTH{1'b0}};
193	reg [NARROW_WIDTH-1:0] bar = NARROW_ZERO;
194	always @(*) begin
195	    bar = WORD_CONSTANT [NARROW_WIDTH-1:0];
196	end
197
198 199

Bit Widths

200 201

Always match bit widths of variable assignments. Even with correct implicit 202 zero/sign-extension, if the source and sink have different width, it will raise 203 a pointless warning in the CAD tool, obscuring other more important warnings. 204 And if the extension is incorrect, it will cause subtle bugs. As before, 205 concatenation and replication can avoid this problem, and then any bit width 206 mismatch warnings become significant. 207 208 

209	// Common
210	
211	localparam SOURCE_WIDTH = 32;
212	localparam SINK_WIDTH   = 16;
213	localparam SOURCE_ZERO  = {SOURCE_WIDTH{1'b0}};
214	localparam SINK_ZERO    = {SINK_WIDTH{1'b0}};
215	
216	reg [SOURCE_WIDTH-1:0] source = SOURCE_ZERO; 
217	reg [SINK_WIDTH-1:0]   sink   = SINK_ZERO;
218	
219	// Zero extension
220	
221	always @(*) begin
222	    sink = {SOURCE_ZERO, source};
223	end
224	
225	// Signed extension
226	
227	reg source_sign = 1'b0;
228	
229	// Not a localparam since it's not a constant value
230	always @(*) begin
231	    source_sign = source[SOURCE_WIDTH-1];
232	    sink        = {{SOURCE_WIDTH{source_sign}}, source};
233	end
234
235 236

Concatenations

237 238

Use concatenations to pack/unpack values instead of selecting ranges of 239 bits, as possible. Concatenating ranges of bits also works. This idiom is very 240 useful to meaningfully extract fields from raw data words (and to concisely 241 document the field format), to group related signals into a single wide 242 pipeline stage register, or to do fixed permutations of bits. 243 244 

245	localparam A_WIDTH      = 1;
246	localparam B_WIDTH      = 2;
247	localparam C_WIDTH      = 3;
248	localparam PACK_WIDTH   = A_WIDTH + B_WIDTH + C_WIDTH;
249	
250	reg [A_WIDTH-1:0]       foo;
251	reg [B_WIDTH-1:0]       bar;
252	reg [C_WIDTH-1:0]       baz;
253	reg [PACK_WIDTH-1:0]    all;
254	
255	// Pack fields into a word
256	always @(*) begin
257	    all = {foo,bar,baz};
258	end
259	
260	// Unpack the fields
261	always @(*) begin
262	    {foo,bar,baz} = all;
263	end
264
265 266

Module Definition

267 268

Parameters

269 270

When defining a module, give all parameters a default value of zero or an 271 empty string, to both tell the user what kind of value is expected, and also 272 make module elaboration fail if any of the parameters are not set at module 273 instantiation. 274 275

Use module parameters to define the bit widths of the module ports, as 276 localparams can only be defined in a module body, which is too late. If a width 277 is globally constant, or calculated from other module parameters and not 278 directly provided by the user, then use another module parameter to compute and 279 hold the value, and place a comment noting that this parameter is not to be 280 used at module instantiation. This is commonly done when a parameterized number 281 of items are passed to a module via concatenation, since Verilog-2001 ports are 282 always present (no conditional instantiation) and simple bit vectors (no 283 arrays of items). 284 285

Ports

286 287

Always define the direction (input, output, inout), type 288 (wire or reg), and name of each module port to reduce 289 boilerplate code, hint at the structure of the module to help the user, and to 290 avoid some synthesis and simulation problems: 291 292

    293 294
  • Defining port direction, type, and name together removes the need to define 295 ports multiple times both in the module port section, then as variables in the 296 module body. This was how it was done in Verilog-1995 and gets very long in 297 large modules and creates useless code you must skip over each time. 298 299
  • All inputs are necessarily wires. If an output is a wire, then it comes 300 from a sub-module instance, which should be the common case in very modular 301 design practice. If an output is a reg, then it comes from some local logic, 302 which indicates a small, special case is handled here. All inout ports are 303 wires, and only used for tri-state I/O at physical pins. 304 305
  • If the port type is not defined, it is possible for different tools to 306 infer different types in some cases, leading to inconsistent synthesis and 307 simulation results. 308 309
310 311

If you have any register output ports, use an initial block 312 immediately after the module port definitions to initialize them to their 313 startup value, as ports cannot be initialized at definition like register 314 variables. 315 316 

317	`default_nettype none
318	
319	module Foo
320	#(
321	    parameter INPUT_WIDTH = 0,
322	    parameter INPUT_COUNT = 0,
323	
324	    // Do not set at instantiation (localparam only legal here in SystemVerilog)
325	    parameter TOTAL_WIDTH = INPUT_WIDTH * INPUT_COUNT,
326	    parameter CONST_WIDTH = `SOME_GLOBAL_WIDTH
327	)
328	(
329	    input  wire                     clock,
330	    input  wire [TOTAL_WIDTH-1:0]   this_input,
331	    output wire [CONST_WIDTH-1:0]   that_output,
332	    output reg                      another_output,
333	    ...
334	);
335	
336	    initial begin
337	        another_output = 1'b0;
338	    end
339	
340	...
341	
342	endmodule
343
344 345

When a module is moderately complicated and the clearest name of an internal 346 signal does not match the module port name it connects to, decouple the names 347 as follows, assuming you already haven't implicitly done so via a final 348 pipeline register stage. 349 350 

351	// Port must be a reg type instead of wire, which denotes a special case to the reader,
352	// and must be initial'ized for proper simulation.
353	always @(*) begin
354	    port_name = internal_name;
355	end
356
357 358

Use port name suffixes to denote if they are inputs or outputs only when 359 there is unavoidable ambiguity (usually for very simple modules with 360 data_in and data_out ports or similar). See a note by 362 @tom_verbeure on why _o and _i port name suffixes 363 can cause problems. 364 365

Vivado limitation in naming

366 367

Although it works in Verilog, and would be a great match for simple modules 368 with well-defined functionality, don't name any ports "in" or "out". Those 369 names are reserved words in VHDL, and Vivado's IP Integrator will reject those 370 names in Verilog source, even if legal. By extension, avoid reserved words in 371 both VHDL and Verilog for naming ports, variables, etc... 372 373

Procedural Blocks and Assignments

374 375 376 377

Assign Statements

378 379

Avoid the use of assign statements, except where demanded by 380 Verilog, as their use makes it hard to see the underlying design, and make the 381 implementation hard to control for size and performance. I have seen assigns 382 used as global variables all over a module, and all too often, as an amorphous 383 large number of assigns to describe the logic followed by a single always block 384 to register all the outputs. (See also Initialization and 385 Logic Values for other assignment cases to avoid.) 386 387

There are exactly two cases where you must use assign: 388 389

    390 391
  1. When inferring a tri-state I/O pin, since the pin must then be a wire, if 392 you are not explicitly instantiating a tri-state I/O buffer (e.g.: IOBUF, 393 OBUFT, etc...). 394 395

    Inferring a tri-state I/O like this may fail if the module is not at the 396 top-level of the design. I have been bitten by this in Vivado, where it created 397 an AND gate instead of a tri-state buffer. Thus, if possible, implement the I/O 398 blocks directly using the CAD vendor modules, which will instantiate correctly 399 regardless of location in the design hierarchy. This is also a reason why 400 FPGA-specific I/O ports should be instantiated 401 in their own enclosing Adapter module. 402 403 

    404	localparam WORD_WIDTH       = 36;
405	localparam WORD_TRISTATE    = {WORD_WIDTH{1'bZ}};
406	
407	reg  [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]   data_out;
408	reg                     output_enable;
409	wire [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]   tristate_bus;
410	
411	assign tristate_bus = (output_enable == 1'b1) ? data_out : WORD_TRISTATE;
412
    413 414
