  2. When dealing with dangling wires. This is a rare case which usually happens 415 when a module in a generate block is conditionally instantiated, 416 and you must connect the dangling wires from the rest of the system to 417 something when it is not instantiated. 418 419
420 421

Always Blocks

422 423

For synchronous designs, there are only two events normally needed to 424 trigger an always block: @(posedge clock) (clocked) 425 and @(*) (combinational). The only use of @(negedge 426 clock) is for capturing incoming I/O data which is sent out on the 427 posedge of the I/O clock, so as to sample the data close the center (in the 428 absence of programmable delay lines on the I/O). The one exception where 429 posedge and negedge logic may be (carefully!) mixed is in Clock Domain Crossing 430 (CDC) circuits. 431 432 433 434

Blocking and Non-Blocking Assignments

435 436

The repeated dogma about Verilog assignments is exactly correct: 437 438

    439
  • Use only blocking assignments (=) in always @(*) (combinational) blocks. 440
  • Use only non-blocking assignments (<=) in always @(posedge clock) blocks. 441
442 443

Blocking assignments should only be used in combinational blocks, despite 444 being legal when used with clocked logic, for two reasons: 445 446

    447 448
  1. The CAD tool may consider the destination of each blocking assignment as an 449 unused register if the value is later used in the same clocked always block 450 (since the value is used before the next clock edge, thus not from the output 451 of the inferred register), which then raises a useless warning. 452 453
  2. The blocking assignment takes effect immediately, rather than at the end 454 of the simulation cycle, so depending on the order in which the simulator 455 evaluates clocked always blocks, there could be race conditions giving 456 different results between simulation runs, or between simulation and synthesis. 457 If you want the deep reasons why, read the IEEE Standard 1364-2001, Section 5, 458 "Scheduling Semantics". (Thanks go to Claire Wolf (@oe1cxw) for enlightening me.). 460 461
462 463

Non-blocking assignments should only be used in clocked always blocks, as 464 simulators may disagree on the interpretation or disallow the usage of 465 non-blocking assignments in combinational always blocks, yielding inconsistent 466 simulations across simulators, and maybe with synthesis results also. For 467 example, see the COMBDLY 469 warning explanation in Verilator. 470 471

Do not mix blocking and non-blocking assignments within an always block. 472 It's legal (unless they assign to the same register!), but not necessary and 473 error-prone. 474 475

For further blocking/non-blocking assignment code examples and explanations 476 (particularly for the Verilog event scheduling model), Clifford E. Cummings' 477 dramatically-titled "Nonblocking 479 Assignments in Verilog Synthesis, Coding Styles That Kill!" is a good, 480 clear read. 481 482

Design Rules for Assignments and Blocks

483 484

Blocking assignments (=) enable a very useful and specific 485 design idiom: to break-down complex logic into simpler expressions we can then 486 think about sequentially and re-use as necessary. 487 488

Backward Dependencies: if you find you have a 489 backwards dependency between blocking assignments (an earlier line depends on a 490 later line), then either re-order the assignments, or split one assignment off 491 into its own always() block. Otherwise, the simulation and 492 synthesis will still be correct, but they may not match anymore. You will see 493 functionally correct behaviour in your simulations that does not match the 494 hardware, such as signals not changing when they should when they otherwise 495 have no effect on the calculations. 496 497

Thus, while simple logic can be correctly computed and assigned in a single 498 line in a clocked always block, it's often clearer to implement more complex 499 logic in a combinational block using blocking assignments and then register the 500 results in an immediately following clocked always block using non-blocking 501 assignments. 502 503

Given the above design rules, it's easy to selectively pipeline logic by 504 having the second always block be clocked or not (and the assignments changed 505 to match), without altering the logic or the layout of the code. It also gives 506 an easy way to estimate how much logic will be placed between registers, and 507 thus get an early grasp on critical paths. 508 509 

510	// Breaking down nested ternary operators into two simpler lines,
511	// with unrelated and parallel logic alongside. 
512	
513	always @(*) begin
514	    part_one        = (cond1 == 1'b1) ? foo : bar;
515	    part_two        = (cond2 == 1'b0) ? baz : part_one;
516	    other_result    = wibble1 ^ wibble2;
517	end
518	
519	// If we don't want to register these values,
520	// simply change the block trigger to @(*),
521	// and the assignments to blocking (=).
522	
523	always @(posedge clock) begin
524	    part_two_reg        <= part_two;
525	    other_result_reg    <= other_result;
526	    another_result      <= blob1 & blob2;
527	end
528
529 530 531 532

Generate Blocks and For Loops

533 534

A generate block is used to iteratively or conditionally instantiate 535 entire modules or always blocks inside of it. For iteration, the index variable 536 must be a genvar, which should be declared inside the generate 537 block for clarity and to avoid re-using it elsewhere. A generate block does 538 not enclose a genvar inside its scope. 539 540

If you need to iteratively create assignments, such as replicating the same 541 logic for multiple ports, then you do not need a generate block, and can use a 542 for loop inside an always block. Doing it this way is also necessary 543 sometimes if the logic may appear to cause conflicting updates to the same 544 variables. Synthesis tools expect all such assignments to be inside the same 545 always block, else the indeterminate scheduling of independent always blocks 546 prevents synthesis, and would make simulation non-deterministic. The index 547 variable of the for loop must be an integer, declared outside the 548 for loop and never re-used elsewhere. 549 550

Do not re-use genvar or integer index variables, as this may not be 551 simulatable. For example, re-using index variables in Icarus led to 552 strange, and silent, simulation failures where for loops ceased to function. I 553 do not know if this is an Icarus bug, or a known behaviour of the Verilog 554 simulation model. Thus, declare each index variable right before the for loop 555 using it, and never re-use it again in any other for loop. 556 557

Logic Design

558 559 560 561

Boolean Expressions

562 563

Express Boolean values behaviourally as equality/inequality tests against 564 the expected value, which clarifies the intent of the code, removes the need to 565 understand the polarity of each logic signal, and makes the bit width explicit, 566 which will avoid some bugs and warnings. If you must invert a comparison, be 567 sure to use the logical negation operator (!), which always 568 returns 1 bit, rather than the bitwise negation (~) of all bits. 569 570 

571	// Rather than this
572	always @(*) begin
573	    C = A & ~B;
574	end
575	
576	// Do this
577	always @(*) begin
578	    C = (A == 1'b1) && (B == 1'b0);
579	end
580
581 582

if/else vs. ternary operators

583 584

Unless otherwise necessary, use ternary operators (?:) instead 585 of if/else statements. There are four main reasons: 586 587

    588 589
  • If a variable is assigned a value in one clause of an if/else, but not the 590 other, a latch may be synthesized. Using a ternary operator makes this error 591 impossible. 592 593
  • Ternary operators are the only way to conditionally assign values to 594 localparams and module parameters. 595 596
  • We can replace nested case/if/else statements with chained lines of ternary 597 operators in blocking assignments, which make the code more compact and easier 598 to follow. But see note above regarding backward dependencies in blocking 599 assignments. 600 601
  • In simulation, Verilog if/else statements treat X and Z values as false, and so 602 will fail to propagate these values and give unexpected results: 603 604 
    605	// Given the following...
606	reg       foo = 1'bX;
607	reg [1:0] bar = 2'b00;
608	
609	// Here, in simulation, foo takes on 2'b10, not 2'bXX
610	always @(*) begin
611	    if (foo == 1b'1) begin
612	        bar = 2'b01;
613	    end
614	    else begin
615	        bar = 2'b10;
616	    end
617	end
618	
619	// Here, in simulation, foo takes 2'bXX
620	always @(*) begin
621	    bar = (foo == 1'b1) ? 2'b01 : 2'b10;
622	end
623
    624 625
626 627

On the other hand, if/else is necessary to conditionally instantiate logic 628 in generate blocks, required by some vendor code templates to 629 infer specific hardware, and unavoidable for some reset code. 630 631 632 633

Chaining Ternary Operators

634 635

Never nest ternary operators, where one term of a ternary operator 636 is itself a ternary operator. That make for unreadable code. Instead, split the 637 logic into two blocking assignments, with the second ternary operator using the 638 output of the first as one of its terms. This style gives you useful 639 intermediate value signals during simulation, extends to an arbitrary number of 640 expressions which we can easily reason about in sequence, and becomes a useful 641 programming pattern for FSMs and other complex logic. 642 643 

644	// Rather than this...
645	always @(*) begin
646	    result = (foo == 1'b1) ? ((bar == 1'b1) ? A : B) : C;
647	end
648	
649	// ...do this!
650	always @(*) begin
651	    partial = (bar == 1'b1) ? A       : B;
652	    result  = (foo == 1'b1) ? partial : C;
653	end
654
655 656

Estimating Logic Usage and Speed

657 658

When desiging logic, keep track of how many unique inputs are needed to 659 generate the output, and match that to the target FPGA Look-Up Tables (LUTs). 660 For example, if the FPGA has 6-input LUTs (6-LUTs), then any logic expression 661 of up to 6 terms can (and usually will) map to a single 6-LUT per bit of output 662 width. If you construct your logic as series of expressions of 6 or fewer terms 663 with registers in between, then you minimize the logic and interconnect delay, 664 and give the CAD tool more freedom to place and route. 665 666

Keeping the number of unique input terms in mind particularly applies to 667 multiplexers. A 4:1 mux has 6 inputs terms (4 input bits and 2 selector bits) 668 and so maps exactly to one 6-LUT per result bit, and can be registered "for 669 free". If you want to maximize speed, be wary of multiplexers wider than 8:1, 670 and avoid designing logic as a single large selection from many options: better 671 to pipeline a sequence of smaller selections. 672 673

I'm glossing over fracturable LUTs and logic packing here, but those are 674 things we can usually take for granted from the CAD tool. 675 676 677 678

State Machines

679 680

Separate your FSM from your data processing, which also enables you to break 681 a larger FSM into smaller ones. For example, each datapath can have its own FSM 682 to perform transactions, and these FSMs can be in turn controlled by another FSM 683 which deals with error-recovery. 684 685

In other words, don't place the state and data processing logic together 686 into a single large case statement, with one case per FSM state, 687 and nested if/else statements inside each case to handle the input/output 688 control signals. Figuring out all possible state transitions and control 689 signals, and eliminating redundant and illegal ones, isn't practical past 690 simple cases, and can lead to lots of seemingly arbitrary code the next reader 691 has to reverse-engineer back into a state diagram. 692 693

Instead, take advantage of the sequential ordering of blocking assignments 694 and of ternary operators, using the idiom of starting with a register and 695 sequentially testing and passing along its updated value in a combinational 696 always block, and registering the updated value in an immediately following 697 clocked always block. If none of the conditions are met, the register remains 698 unchanged. 699 700

Then, using this idiom, describe the possible states of the datapath 701 (independent of control), build up definitions of the basic datapath operations 702 (independent of state) and of the allowed transformations on the datapath (as 703 operations and the states they may occur in), and then express the state 704 transition and control signal logic in terms of those datapath transformations. 705 This approach describes the FSM at a higher level and reduces code duplication, 706 making it easier to read, debug, and maintain. Expect to need a larger than 707 usual number of comments in your FSM, as it depends on an external context (the 708 datapath) to be meaningful. 709 710

To see these idioms used to create a small but non-trivial FSM, have a look 711 at the Skid Buffer module. 712 713 714 715

Resets

716 717

(Resets are one place where FPGA and ASIC design practices diverge. You 718 are also better off encapsulating all the issues discussed below inside a Register module) 720 721

Make use of the implicit power-on-reset in FPGAs, limit the number of things 722 which need an explicit asynchronous reset signal, and feed reset from a signal 723 synchronous to the clock. This means passing an external reset through a CDC Bit Synchronizer and ensuring a 725 sufficient reset time. 726 727

On FPGAs, the hardware reset of a flip-flop is usually asynchronous and so 728 takes effect immediately rather than at the next clock edge, which can 729 cause subtle bugs: a register appears to fail to capture data in 730 behavioural simulation, or changes in impossible ways (within less than 731 a clock cycle) in timing-annotated post-synthesis simulation. 732 733

If you need to reset during normal operation, use a separate synchronous 734 "clear" signal controlling the data into the flip-flop. This may create extra 735 logic, but that logic gets folded into other logic feeding data to the 736 register, and would have been necessary anyway. 737 738

True asynchronous resets are an exception when a clock isn't yet available 739 (e.g.: PLL reset), or when the synchronous control logic is wedged. 740 741

The configuration bitstream of an FPGA includes the initial state of all 742 registers and (most) on-chip memories, so most logic does not require a reset 743 signal to properly start operating. See Ken Chapman's Get 745 Smart About Reset: Think Local, Not Global for details. Set the initial 746 state of registers by assigning it at declaration, or assigning it inside an 747 initial block for module register output ports. Most on-chip 748 memories can be similarly initialized with a known content via a 749 $readmemh() directive, or some code in an initial 750 block. Refer to the Single Port RAM code 751 for an example of on-chip memory initialization. 752 753

Resets on Different FPGA Families

754 755 The following was contributed by @ravenslofty (edited by me): 757 758
759
760 Perhaps a better point against reg initialisation at declaration is its 761 inconsistency across chips - not all FPGAs support all combinations of init and 762 reset. FPGA flops can very roughly be split into four categories: 763 764
    765
  • not initialisable (QuickLogic EOS S3) 766
  • constant zero (Intel Cyclone V; Lattice iCE40) 767
  • fully configurable (Xilinx 7 Series) 768
  • init-matches-set (Lattice ECP5) 769
770 771 This means you can write a register init declaration which, when combined with 772 an asynchronous set/reset wire, is impossible to directly satisfy in hardware. 773 However, synchronous sets/resets can be emulated via input logic, so a global 774 synchronous reset can always be implemented. 775
776
777 778 Fortunately, asynchronous resets to registers are rare, and not a good thing in 779 general (they inhibits register retiming, which enables simpler, faster 780 designs). So register initialization still works, except where you need an 781 explicit asynchronous reset. 782 783 784

The "Last Assignment Wins" Reset Idiom

785 786

The common idiom for resets uses an if/else statement, where each 787 register must be assigned a value in both clauses (else a latch may be 788 synthesized), which means all registers must be reset, maximizing the size of 789 the reset tree, which uses more routing and makes timing harder to meet. 790 791 

792	// Common idiom, which has a problem...
793	always @(posedge clock) begin
794	    if (reset == 1'b1) begin
795	        foo <= FOO_RESET;
796	        bar <= BAR_RESET; // but does not need a reset!
797	    end
798	    else begin
799	        foo <= foo_next;
800	        bar <= bar_next;
801	    end
802	end
803
804 805

If you must reset some registers, use one of the following idioms instead to 806 minimize the size of the reset tree: 807 808 

809	// Ternary operator assignment for reset
810	always @(posedge clock) begin
811	    foo <= (reset == 1'b1) ? FOO_RESET : foo_next;
812	    bar <= bar_next
813	end
814	
815	// Using "last assignment wins" semantics for reset
816	always @(posedge clock) begin
817	    foo <= foo_next;
818	    bar <= bar_next;
819	
820	    if (reset == 1'b1) begin
821	        foo <= FOO_RESET;
822	    end
823	end
824
825 826

Credit for the "last assignment wins" idiom goes to Olof Kindgren (@olofkindgren): Resetting 829 reset handling. This idiom also applies 831 to VHDL. 832 833

Alse see this twitter 835 thread which discusses a subtlety of "last assignment wins" which explains 836 a subtlety of non-blocking assignments and ternary operators. Credit to 837 Claire Wolf (@oe1cxw). 838 839

Combining Asynchronous Reset and Clock Enable

840 841

On FPGAs, the flip-flop reset hardware is asynchronous, and this causes 842 difficulties when inferring flip-flops with clock_enables from Verilog code. 843 The "last assignment wins" idiom to implement reset doesn't work here: having 844 two separate if-statements (one for clock_enable followed by one for reset) 845 does not work when the reset is asynchronously specified alongside the clock in 846 the sensitivity list (as shown below), as there is no way to determine which 847 signal in the sensitivity list each if statement should respond to. 848 849

Thus, correct flip-flop hardware inference depends on explicitly expressing 850 the priority of the reset over the clock_enable structurally with nested 851 if-statements, rather than implicitly through the Verilog event queue via the 852 "last assignment wins" idiom. 853 854

This is very likely the *only* place you will ever need an asynchronous 855 signal in a sensitivity list, or express explicit structural priority. 856 857

Also, even though the flip-flop reset hardware is asynchronous, it should be 858 fed by a synchronous reset signal, otherwise if the flip-flop was not already 859 at zero, it may flip to zero at a time such that the signal change reaches the 860 next flip-flops down the line within their metastability window. 861 862 

863	`default_nettype none
864	
865	module Register
866	#(
867	    parameter WORD_WIDTH  = 0,
868	    parameter RESET_VALUE = 0
869	)
870	(
871	    input   wire                        clock,
872	    input   wire                        clock_enable,
873	    input   wire                        areset,
874	    input   wire                        clear,
875	    input   wire    [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    data_in,
876	    output  reg     [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    data_out
877	);
878	
879	    initial begin
880	        data_out = RESET_VALUE;
881	    end
882	
883	    reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] selected = RESET_VALUE;
884	
885	    always @(*) begin
886	        selected = (clear == 1'b1) ? RESET_VALUE : data_in;
887	    end
888	
889	    always @(posedge clock or posedge areset) begin
890	        if (areset == 1'b1) begin
891	            data_out <= RESET_VALUE;
892	        end
893	        else begin
894	            if ((clock_enable == 1'b1) || (clear == 1'b1)) begin
895	                data_out <= selected;
896	            end
897	        end
898	    end
899	
900	endmodule
901
902 903

Reset Sequencing

904 905

Various parts of a design may have to stay in reset for a minimum amount of 906 time to properly initialize (e.g.: 200us for DDR2 RAM), and have to come out of 907 reset in a certain order to avoid receiving undefined signals. You can sequence 908 the resets by creating delayed copies of the power-on-reset with a few counters 909 driven by the main clock, with the count values set as top-level design module 910 parameters. 911 912

Some Corner Cases

913 914 915 916

Simulated Clock Generation

917 918

In simulation, a race condition can exist at time zero between the initial 919 value assignment of a register and the first clock edge. For example: 920 921 

922	reg clock = 1'b0; // Counts as a negedge at time zero! (X -> 0)
923	reg foo   = 1'b0; // Also does X -> 0 at time zero.
924	
925	// Simulate the clock
926	always begin
927	    #`HALF_PERIOD clock = ~clock;
928	end
929	
930	// It is unclear if the clock edge or the "foo" initialization will happen first,
931	// so "bar" can get X for one simulation cycle...
932	always @(negedge clock) begin
933	    bar <= foo;
934	end
935
936 937

This race condition is another reason to only use @(posedge 938 clock) in internal logic, but the same race condition will happen if the 939 simulation clock happens to be initialized to 1'b1. 940 941

Instead, the following clock generation idiom (credit: Claire Wolf (@oe1cxw)) avoids the race condition by 943 making use of undefined values and the identity operator (===) 944 instead of the equality (==) operator: 945 946 

947	// NOTE: clock is left uninitialized, and thus X in most simulators, and will
948	// not trigger a (X -> 0) edge until after the simulated clock half-period delay.
949	always begin
950	    #`HALF_PERIOD clock = (clock === 1'b0);
951	end
952
953 954

The Simulation Clock module implements 955 this idiom. 956 957

Constant Values

958 959

When a combinational procedural block contains only constant values on the 960 right hand side, your linter and/or simulator may raise warnings. For example: 961 962 

963	reg foo;
964	always @(*) begin
965	    foo = 1'b0;
966	end
967
968 969

Since there can be no clock edge event or value change event of a right hand 970 side value to trigger the procedural block, it is possible that the assignment 971 never happens, depending on the interpretation of the CAD tool. This example is 972 contrived, but such a situation will occur if you have a module output port 973 which is both a register and outputs a constant. Since you cannot initialize a 974 register in a port definition, you must instead set the constant value in an 975 initial block, or at an extreme, change the reg port 976 to a wire and assign it a constant value. (But this 977 is strongly discouraged.) (This corner case was pointed out (and fixed!) by 978 Rodrigo Melo.) 979 980 981 982

Avoiding Zero-Width Padding

983 984

When splitting a long bit vector into a number of words of a given width, or 985 concatenating multiple words into a single wider vector, it's possible that you 986 will need to pad a few unused bits, usually with zeros. Typically, you compute 987 the width difference or the division remainder to get the pad width, then 988 create the pad with a replication: 989 990 

991	localparam PAD_WIDTH   = BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH % WORD_WIDTH;
992	localparam TOTAL_WIDTH = WORD_WIDTH * WORD_COUNT;        // Alternate
993	localparam PAD_WIDTH   = BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH - TOTAL_WIDTH; // Alternate
994	localparam PAD         = {PAD_WIDTH{1'b0}};
995
996 997

However, this fails if no pad is needed. Variables of zero width cannot 998 exist in Verilog, and a zero width replication is also illegal in Verilog-2001. 999 Zero width concatenations are partially fixed in Verilog-2005, and thus 1000 SystemVerilog, but the above PAD would still fail at linting and 1001 elaboration since there are no other sized elements being concatenated. 1002 1003

We can eliminate zero from the possible PAD_WIDTH values by 1004 instead returning WORD_WIDTH if the pad width would have been 1005 zero. This works because a pad is necessarily between 0 and 1006 WORD_WIDTH-1 bits wide. We can then check later and instantiate a 1007 concatenation with or without a pad: 1008 1009 

1010	reg [BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH-1:0] baz;
1011	reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]       wibble, ...;
1012	
1013	generate
1014	    if (PAD_WIDTH != WORD_WIDTH) begin
1015	        always @(*) begin
1016	            baz = {PAD, wibble, ....}; // Padding MSBs here
1017	        end
1018	    end
1019	    else begin
1020	        always @(*) begin
1021	            baz = {wibble, ... };
1022	        end
1023	    end
1024	endgenerate
1025
1026 1027

The Word Pad function implements this 1028 idiom, returning the pad width or `WORD_WIDTH` if the pad width would be zero. 1029 1030

Simple Cases for Zero-Padding

1031 1032

While the Word Pad function enables 1033 padding of arbitrary length at arbitrary positions (e.g.: you could pad in the 1034 middle of a wide vector with a special pattern instead of only zeros), the 1035 common case is to pad with zeros at the least-significant bits. 1036 1037

We can zero-pad the least significant bits of a wider vector automatically 1038 by making sure the vector is initialized to zero, then using a vector part 1039 select to place the concatenated words into the wider vector at just after the 1040 location of the pad. This approach automatically handles the case where the pad 1041 width equals zero without defining impossible zero-width variables or 1042 replications. 1043 1044 

1045	localparam BIG_VECTOR_ZERO = {BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH{1'b0}};
1046	localparam TOTAL_WIDTH     = WORD_WIDTH * WORD_COUNT;
1047	localparam PAD_WIDTH       = BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH - TOTAL_WIDTH;
1048	
1049	reg [BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH-1:0] baz = BIG_VECTOR_ZERO;
1050	reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]       wibble, ...;
1051	
1052	always @(*) begin
1053	    baz [PAD_WIDTH +: TOTAL_WIDTH] = {wibble, ... };
1054	end
1055
1056 1057

Alternately, you could first concatenate the individual words into a vector 1058 of TOTAL_WIDTH bits, then pass it through a Width Adjuster to widen it to 1060 BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH, then pass that wider vector through a Bit Shifter to shift it left by 1062 PAD_WIDTH, filling in the least-significant bits with any pattern 1063 you wish. 1064 1065

1066 1067 fpgacpu.ca 1068

file.content:
Verilog Coding Standard

from FPGA Design Elements by Charles Eric LaForest, PhD.

This is a continually evolving document. Please send any feedback to eric@fpgacpu.ca or @elaforest or join the Discord server.

Over time, I've found that most of the difficulty with Verilog is a problem with programming practice which goes away with a certain coding style, which I describe here. It's not perfect, but it works and makes Verilog programming easier, more pleasant, and more reliable.

It matters to have a Verilog coding standard, because Verilog doesn't matter. We want to quickly get past the nuts-and-bolts of Verilog itself so we can focus on the system we are trying to build. I describe the related system design practice in the companion System Design Standard.

Scope

This standard was written with FPGAs in mind so some details may not apply, or may be missing, in relation to ASICs. Nonetheless, the standard should still largely be applicable to ASIC design.

This standard mainly defines a restricted form of Verilog which uses a limited number of programming idioms. These restrictions reduce bugs, make the code easier to understand, and aim to give consistent synthesis results across CAD tools, whether the code is manually written or machine-generated.

This standard does not define any typesetting conventions such as brace style, indentation, variable naming, comment format, etc... You will see code examples typeset in a style I recommend, but it is not essential. Like regular written language prose, anything that helps readability is good.

Nor does this standard define any testing, simulation, verification, or validation methodologies (though I hope you have some!). However, it will address some pitfalls and low-level details.

Finally, this standard assumes you are already familiar with Verilog and digital design in general. It is not a language or tool reference.

Verilog Language Versions

Use Verilog-2001, specifically its synthesizable subset, as it's well supported across CAD tools. Anything not synthesizable is for simulation/verification and outside the scope of this standard.

The previous version, Verilog-1995, lacks several useful features (named port connections, vector part select, generate blocks, etc...) without which your code will get longer and more difficult. Avoid mixing Verilog-1995 forms into Verilog-2001 code, despite the backwards compatibility.

The following version, SystemVerilog, brings in too many features and thus has uneven support across CAD tools. It does have desirable features (interfaces, parameterizable port instances, structs, enums, etc...), but until support matures and a similarly restricted, synthesizable subset can be agreed upon, it should be avoided or used for simulation/verification (which it is well suited for!).

Definitions and Inclusions

Use `include directives only to bring in common function definitions which, due to a limitation of Verilog-2001, must be defined inside the body of a module. A common example is an integer logarithm function to calculate address width. Place the function in a separate file and include it at the start of the body of modules which need it.

Use `define directives only for values which remain valid and constant everywhere and always in the design, don't depend on any other less-constant values, and can't be passed as module parameters (e.g.: opcode encodings for ALU functional units). Place these definitions into a separate file and `include it at the beginning of files which need them. Otherwise, construct constants within a module using localparam.

Basic Types and Values

All variables, modules, constants, etc... must be defined before they are used in a file, going from top to bottom. If you have a wire connecting the output of a later module to the input of an earlier module, define the wire first, before the earliest module using it.

Defaults

Use `default_nettype none at the start of each file, before module definition. This causes any undefined variable to be an error. Otherwise, accidentally undefined variables become 1-bit wires, which may cause subtle bugs during synthesis.

For code you cannot control which assumes a default net type (e.g. vendor-provided models and IP blocks), wrap the code as follows inside the provided source file: 

`default_nettype wire

...

`default_nettype none

Initialization and Logic Values

Use only reg and wire types. All signals are registers except where a wire is mandatory: module input ports, connecting module ports together, and inferring tri-state I/O. (See Assign Statements for details.)

Only use 0,1,X,Z values, and avoid X and Z if at all possible. Do not assign X to registers or wires, as X propagation will make testing difficult, and isn't supported in 2-state simulators (e.g.: Verilator). Instead, have a default catch-all or fall-through logic expression which takes effect when all others don't. The same idea applies to Boolean tests. Z is only used to define tri-state I/O, and only if the CAD vendor does not provide tri-state I/O primitives, as the inference is unreliable.

All registers must be initialized (and reset, if necessary) with a value which does not contain X or Z, either at definition or using an initial block. Note that some combinations of register reset and initialization may not work well together depending on the FPGA family. See Resets for details.

Never assign an initial value to wires, either using an explicit assign statement or an implicit initial assignment (e.g.: wire foo = bar;). Assigning a wire an initial value can cause problem if you then mistakenly connect that wire to a module port output, leading to multiple drivers on the wire and surprise X's in your simulation. This situation easily happens if you convert a reg to a wire, because the signal source is now in a module, and you forget to remove the initial assignment on the reg (usually a zero).

Parameterization

Wherever possible, parameterize the width of your variables rather than using constants. It's more work initially, but makes modules more general and avoids tedious and error-prone edits when something inevitably changes. If the width can be set by the user, then use a module parameter. If it's an internal module constant, use a localparam. Very, very rarely, use a `define constant. Never use a hardcoded literal integer: name it with a localparam instead.

Unfortunately, in Verilog-2001, module parameters and localparams are not valid width specifiers for literal values, only `define constants and literal integers are. This makes initializing registers of parameterized width with values of the exact same width impossible, and it's unclear if the implicit width-extension for values like 'b0 or 'd1 work correctly past 32 or 64 bits width, depending on the CAD tool. As a workaround, use concatenation and replication to create the value you need of the proper bit width.

Also note that localparam is not legal in the ports definition of a module definition. That's only in SystemVerilog. Thus, if you need a constant parameter for a port width or some other parameter calculation, you have to use a parameter, and leave a comment that it should not be set at module instantiation. 

// This is 32-bits by default
localparam WORD_WIDTH = 72;

// Also legal to define bit width
localparam [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] WORD_CONSTANT = `SOME_DEFINED_CONSTANT;

// Not legal
reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] foo = WORD_WIDTH'b0;

// Legal workarounds
localparam WORD_ZERO      = {WORD_WIDTH{1'b0}};
localparam WORD_ONE       = {{WORD_WIDTH-1{1'b0}},1'b1};
localparam WORD_MINUS_ONE = ~WORD_ZERO;

reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] foo  = WORD_ZERO;

// Simpler, but unclear if extended with zeros or Xs past 32 or 64 bits
reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] foo = 'b0;
reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] foo = 'd42;

// Sometimes you have to clip at the point of use
localparam NARROW_ZERO = {NARROW_WIDTH{1'b0}};
reg [NARROW_WIDTH-1:0] bar = NARROW_ZERO;
always @(*) begin
    bar = WORD_CONSTANT [NARROW_WIDTH-1:0];
end

Bit Widths

Always match bit widths of variable assignments. Even with correct implicit zero/sign-extension, if the source and sink have different width, it will raise a pointless warning in the CAD tool, obscuring other more important warnings. And if the extension is incorrect, it will cause subtle bugs. As before, concatenation and replication can avoid this problem, and then any bit width mismatch warnings become significant. 

// Common

localparam SOURCE_WIDTH = 32;
localparam SINK_WIDTH   = 16;
localparam SOURCE_ZERO  = {SOURCE_WIDTH{1'b0}};
localparam SINK_ZERO    = {SINK_WIDTH{1'b0}};

reg [SOURCE_WIDTH-1:0] source = SOURCE_ZERO; 
reg [SINK_WIDTH-1:0]   sink   = SINK_ZERO;

// Zero extension

always @(*) begin
    sink = {SOURCE_ZERO, source};
end

// Signed extension

reg source_sign = 1'b0;

// Not a localparam since it's not a constant value
always @(*) begin
    source_sign = source[SOURCE_WIDTH-1];
    sink        = {{SOURCE_WIDTH{source_sign}}, source};
end

Concatenations

Use concatenations to pack/unpack values instead of selecting ranges of bits, as possible. Concatenating ranges of bits also works. This idiom is very useful to meaningfully extract fields from raw data words (and to concisely document the field format), to group related signals into a single wide pipeline stage register, or to do fixed permutations of bits. 

localparam A_WIDTH      = 1;
localparam B_WIDTH      = 2;
localparam C_WIDTH      = 3;
localparam PACK_WIDTH   = A_WIDTH + B_WIDTH + C_WIDTH;

reg [A_WIDTH-1:0]       foo;
reg [B_WIDTH-1:0]       bar;
reg [C_WIDTH-1:0]       baz;
reg [PACK_WIDTH-1:0]    all;

// Pack fields into a word
always @(*) begin
    all = {foo,bar,baz};
end

// Unpack the fields
always @(*) begin
    {foo,bar,baz} = all;
end

Module Definition

Parameters

When defining a module, give all parameters a default value of zero or an empty string, to both tell the user what kind of value is expected, and also make module elaboration fail if any of the parameters are not set at module instantiation.

Use module parameters to define the bit widths of the module ports, as localparams can only be defined in a module body, which is too late. If a width is globally constant, or calculated from other module parameters and not directly provided by the user, then use another module parameter to compute and hold the value, and place a comment noting that this parameter is not to be used at module instantiation. This is commonly done when a parameterized number of items are passed to a module via concatenation, since Verilog-2001 ports are always present (no conditional instantiation) and simple bit vectors (no arrays of items).

Ports

Always define the direction (input, output, inout), type (wire or reg), and name of each module port to reduce boilerplate code, hint at the structure of the module to help the user, and to avoid some synthesis and simulation problems:

  • Defining port direction, type, and name together removes the need to define ports multiple times both in the module port section, then as variables in the module body. This was how it was done in Verilog-1995 and gets very long in large modules and creates useless code you must skip over each time.
  • All inputs are necessarily wires. If an output is a wire, then it comes from a sub-module instance, which should be the common case in very modular design practice. If an output is a reg, then it comes from some local logic, which indicates a small, special case is handled here. All inout ports are wires, and only used for tri-state I/O at physical pins.
  • If the port type is not defined, it is possible for different tools to infer different types in some cases, leading to inconsistent synthesis and simulation results.

If you have any register output ports, use an initial block immediately after the module port definitions to initialize them to their startup value, as ports cannot be initialized at definition like register variables. 

`default_nettype none

module Foo
#(
    parameter INPUT_WIDTH = 0,
    parameter INPUT_COUNT = 0,

    // Do not set at instantiation (localparam only legal here in SystemVerilog)
    parameter TOTAL_WIDTH = INPUT_WIDTH * INPUT_COUNT,
    parameter CONST_WIDTH = `SOME_GLOBAL_WIDTH
)
(
    input  wire                     clock,
    input  wire [TOTAL_WIDTH-1:0]   this_input,
    output wire [CONST_WIDTH-1:0]   that_output,
    output reg                      another_output,
    ...
);

    initial begin
        another_output = 1'b0;
    end

...

endmodule

When a module is moderately complicated and the clearest name of an internal signal does not match the module port name it connects to, decouple the names as follows, assuming you already haven't implicitly done so via a final pipeline register stage. 

// Port must be a reg type instead of wire, which denotes a special case to the reader,
// and must be initial'ized for proper simulation.
always @(*) begin
    port_name = internal_name;
end

Use port name suffixes to denote if they are inputs or outputs only when there is unavoidable ambiguity (usually for very simple modules with data_in and data_out ports or similar). See a note by @tom_verbeure on why _o and _i port name suffixes can cause problems.

Vivado limitation in naming

Although it works in Verilog, and would be a great match for simple modules with well-defined functionality, don't name any ports "in" or "out". Those names are reserved words in VHDL, and Vivado's IP Integrator will reject those names in Verilog source, even if legal. By extension, avoid reserved words in both VHDL and Verilog for naming ports, variables, etc...

Procedural Blocks and Assignments

Assign Statements

Avoid the use of assign statements, except where demanded by Verilog, as their use makes it hard to see the underlying design, and make the implementation hard to control for size and performance. I have seen assigns used as global variables all over a module, and all too often, as an amorphous large number of assigns to describe the logic followed by a single always block to register all the outputs. (See also Initialization and Logic Values for other assignment cases to avoid.)

There are exactly two cases where you must use assign:

  1. When inferring a tri-state I/O pin, since the pin must then be a wire, if you are not explicitly instantiating a tri-state I/O buffer (e.g.: IOBUF, OBUFT, etc...).

    Inferring a tri-state I/O like this may fail if the module is not at the top-level of the design. I have been bitten by this in Vivado, where it created an AND gate instead of a tri-state buffer. Thus, if possible, implement the I/O blocks directly using the CAD vendor modules, which will instantiate correctly regardless of location in the design hierarchy. This is also a reason why FPGA-specific I/O ports should be instantiated in their own enclosing Adapter module. 

    localparam WORD_WIDTH       = 36;
localparam WORD_TRISTATE    = {WORD_WIDTH{1'bZ}};

reg  [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]   data_out;
reg                     output_enable;
wire [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]   tristate_bus;

assign tristate_bus = (output_enable == 1'b1) ? data_out : WORD_TRISTATE;
  2. When dealing with dangling wires. This is a rare case which usually happens when a module in a generate block is conditionally instantiated, and you must connect the dangling wires from the rest of the system to something when it is not instantiated.

Always Blocks

For synchronous designs, there are only two events normally needed to trigger an always block: @(posedge clock) (clocked) and @(*) (combinational). The only use of @(negedge clock) is for capturing incoming I/O data which is sent out on the posedge of the I/O clock, so as to sample the data close the center (in the absence of programmable delay lines on the I/O). The one exception where posedge and negedge logic may be (carefully!) mixed is in Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) circuits.

Blocking and Non-Blocking Assignments

The repeated dogma about Verilog assignments is exactly correct:

  • Use only blocking assignments (=) in always @(*) (combinational) blocks.
  • Use only non-blocking assignments (<=) in always @(posedge clock) blocks.

Blocking assignments should only be used in combinational blocks, despite being legal when used with clocked logic, for two reasons:

  1. The CAD tool may consider the destination of each blocking assignment as an unused register if the value is later used in the same clocked always block (since the value is used before the next clock edge, thus not from the output of the inferred register), which then raises a useless warning.
  2. The blocking assignment takes effect immediately, rather than at the end of the simulation cycle, so depending on the order in which the simulator evaluates clocked always blocks, there could be race conditions giving different results between simulation runs, or between simulation and synthesis. If you want the deep reasons why, read the IEEE Standard 1364-2001, Section 5, "Scheduling Semantics". (Thanks go to Claire Wolf (@oe1cxw) for enlightening me.).

Non-blocking assignments should only be used in clocked always blocks, as simulators may disagree on the interpretation or disallow the usage of non-blocking assignments in combinational always blocks, yielding inconsistent simulations across simulators, and maybe with synthesis results also. For example, see the COMBDLY warning explanation in Verilator.

Do not mix blocking and non-blocking assignments within an always block. It's legal (unless they assign to the same register!), but not necessary and error-prone.

For further blocking/non-blocking assignment code examples and explanations (particularly for the Verilog event scheduling model), Clifford E. Cummings' dramatically-titled "Nonblocking Assignments in Verilog Synthesis, Coding Styles That Kill!" is a good, clear read.

Design Rules for Assignments and Blocks

Blocking assignments (=) enable a very useful and specific design idiom: to break-down complex logic into simpler expressions we can then think about sequentially and re-use as necessary.

Backward Dependencies: if you find you have a backwards dependency between blocking assignments (an earlier line depends on a later line), then either re-order the assignments, or split one assignment off into its own always() block. Otherwise, the simulation and synthesis will still be correct, but they may not match anymore. You will see functionally correct behaviour in your simulations that does not match the hardware, such as signals not changing when they should when they otherwise have no effect on the calculations.

Thus, while simple logic can be correctly computed and assigned in a single line in a clocked always block, it's often clearer to implement more complex logic in a combinational block using blocking assignments and then register the results in an immediately following clocked always block using non-blocking assignments.

Given the above design rules, it's easy to selectively pipeline logic by having the second always block be clocked or not (and the assignments changed to match), without altering the logic or the layout of the code. It also gives an easy way to estimate how much logic will be placed between registers, and thus get an early grasp on critical paths. 

// Breaking down nested ternary operators into two simpler lines,
// with unrelated and parallel logic alongside. 

always @(*) begin
    part_one        = (cond1 == 1'b1) ? foo : bar;
    part_two        = (cond2 == 1'b0) ? baz : part_one;
    other_result    = wibble1 ^ wibble2;
end

// If we don't want to register these values,
// simply change the block trigger to @(*),
// and the assignments to blocking (=).

always @(posedge clock) begin
    part_two_reg        <= part_two;
    other_result_reg    <= other_result;
    another_result      <= blob1 & blob2;
end

Generate Blocks and For Loops

A generate block is used to iteratively or conditionally instantiate entire modules or always blocks inside of it. For iteration, the index variable must be a genvar, which should be declared inside the generate block for clarity and to avoid re-using it elsewhere. A generate block does not enclose a genvar inside its scope.

If you need to iteratively create assignments, such as replicating the same logic for multiple ports, then you do not need a generate block, and can use a for loop inside an always block. Doing it this way is also necessary sometimes if the logic may appear to cause conflicting updates to the same variables. Synthesis tools expect all such assignments to be inside the same always block, else the indeterminate scheduling of independent always blocks prevents synthesis, and would make simulation non-deterministic. The index variable of the for loop must be an integer, declared outside the for loop and never re-used elsewhere.

Do not re-use genvar or integer index variables, as this may not be simulatable. For example, re-using index variables in Icarus led to strange, and silent, simulation failures where for loops ceased to function. I do not know if this is an Icarus bug, or a known behaviour of the Verilog simulation model. Thus, declare each index variable right before the for loop using it, and never re-use it again in any other for loop.

Logic Design

Boolean Expressions

Express Boolean values behaviourally as equality/inequality tests against the expected value, which clarifies the intent of the code, removes the need to understand the polarity of each logic signal, and makes the bit width explicit, which will avoid some bugs and warnings. If you must invert a comparison, be sure to use the logical negation operator (!), which always returns 1 bit, rather than the bitwise negation (~) of all bits. 

// Rather than this
always @(*) begin
    C = A & ~B;
end

// Do this
always @(*) begin
    C = (A == 1'b1) && (B == 1'b0);
end

if/else vs. ternary operators

Unless otherwise necessary, use ternary operators (?:) instead of if/else statements. There are four main reasons:

  • If a variable is assigned a value in one clause of an if/else, but not the other, a latch may be synthesized. Using a ternary operator makes this error impossible.
  • Ternary operators are the only way to conditionally assign values to localparams and module parameters.
  • We can replace nested case/if/else statements with chained lines of ternary operators in blocking assignments, which make the code more compact and easier to follow. But see note above regarding backward dependencies in blocking assignments.
  • In simulation, Verilog if/else statements treat X and Z values as false, and so will fail to propagate these values and give unexpected results: 
    // Given the following...
reg       foo = 1'bX;
reg [1:0] bar = 2'b00;

// Here, in simulation, foo takes on 2'b10, not 2'bXX
always @(*) begin
    if (foo == 1b'1) begin
        bar = 2'b01;
    end
    else begin
        bar = 2'b10;
    end
end

// Here, in simulation, foo takes 2'bXX
always @(*) begin
    bar = (foo == 1'b1) ? 2'b01 : 2'b10;
end

On the other hand, if/else is necessary to conditionally instantiate logic in generate blocks, required by some vendor code templates to infer specific hardware, and unavoidable for some reset code.

Chaining Ternary Operators

Never nest ternary operators, where one term of a ternary operator is itself a ternary operator. That make for unreadable code. Instead, split the logic into two blocking assignments, with the second ternary operator using the output of the first as one of its terms. This style gives you useful intermediate value signals during simulation, extends to an arbitrary number of expressions which we can easily reason about in sequence, and becomes a useful programming pattern for FSMs and other complex logic. 

// Rather than this...
always @(*) begin
    result = (foo == 1'b1) ? ((bar == 1'b1) ? A : B) : C;
end

// ...do this!
always @(*) begin
    partial = (bar == 1'b1) ? A       : B;
    result  = (foo == 1'b1) ? partial : C;
end

Estimating Logic Usage and Speed

When desiging logic, keep track of how many unique inputs are needed to generate the output, and match that to the target FPGA Look-Up Tables (LUTs). For example, if the FPGA has 6-input LUTs (6-LUTs), then any logic expression of up to 6 terms can (and usually will) map to a single 6-LUT per bit of output width. If you construct your logic as series of expressions of 6 or fewer terms with registers in between, then you minimize the logic and interconnect delay, and give the CAD tool more freedom to place and route.

Keeping the number of unique input terms in mind particularly applies to multiplexers. A 4:1 mux has 6 inputs terms (4 input bits and 2 selector bits) and so maps exactly to one 6-LUT per result bit, and can be registered "for free". If you want to maximize speed, be wary of multiplexers wider than 8:1, and avoid designing logic as a single large selection from many options: better to pipeline a sequence of smaller selections.

I'm glossing over fracturable LUTs and logic packing here, but those are things we can usually take for granted from the CAD tool.

State Machines

Separate your FSM from your data processing, which also enables you to break a larger FSM into smaller ones. For example, each datapath can have its own FSM to perform transactions, and these FSMs can be in turn controlled by another FSM which deals with error-recovery.

In other words, don't place the state and data processing logic together into a single large case statement, with one case per FSM state, and nested if/else statements inside each case to handle the input/output control signals. Figuring out all possible state transitions and control signals, and eliminating redundant and illegal ones, isn't practical past simple cases, and can lead to lots of seemingly arbitrary code the next reader has to reverse-engineer back into a state diagram.

Instead, take advantage of the sequential ordering of blocking assignments and of ternary operators, using the idiom of starting with a register and sequentially testing and passing along its updated value in a combinational always block, and registering the updated value in an immediately following clocked always block. If none of the conditions are met, the register remains unchanged.

Then, using this idiom, describe the possible states of the datapath (independent of control), build up definitions of the basic datapath operations (independent of state) and of the allowed transformations on the datapath (as operations and the states they may occur in), and then express the state transition and control signal logic in terms of those datapath transformations. This approach describes the FSM at a higher level and reduces code duplication, making it easier to read, debug, and maintain. Expect to need a larger than usual number of comments in your FSM, as it depends on an external context (the datapath) to be meaningful.

To see these idioms used to create a small but non-trivial FSM, have a look at the Skid Buffer module.

Resets

(Resets are one place where FPGA and ASIC design practices diverge. You are also better off encapsulating all the issues discussed below inside a Register module)

Make use of the implicit power-on-reset in FPGAs, limit the number of things which need an explicit asynchronous reset signal, and feed reset from a signal synchronous to the clock. This means passing an external reset through a CDC Bit Synchronizer and ensuring a sufficient reset time.

On FPGAs, the hardware reset of a flip-flop is usually asynchronous and so takes effect immediately rather than at the next clock edge, which can cause subtle bugs: a register appears to fail to capture data in behavioural simulation, or changes in impossible ways (within less than a clock cycle) in timing-annotated post-synthesis simulation.

If you need to reset during normal operation, use a separate synchronous "clear" signal controlling the data into the flip-flop. This may create extra logic, but that logic gets folded into other logic feeding data to the register, and would have been necessary anyway.

True asynchronous resets are an exception when a clock isn't yet available (e.g.: PLL reset), or when the synchronous control logic is wedged.

The configuration bitstream of an FPGA includes the initial state of all registers and (most) on-chip memories, so most logic does not require a reset signal to properly start operating. See Ken Chapman's Get Smart About Reset: Think Local, Not Global for details. Set the initial state of registers by assigning it at declaration, or assigning it inside an initial block for module register output ports. Most on-chip memories can be similarly initialized with a known content via a $readmemh() directive, or some code in an initial block. Refer to the Single Port RAM code for an example of on-chip memory initialization.

Resets on Different FPGA Families

The following was contributed by @ravenslofty (edited by me):
Perhaps a better point against reg initialisation at declaration is its inconsistency across chips - not all FPGAs support all combinations of init and reset. FPGA flops can very roughly be split into four categories:
  • not initialisable (QuickLogic EOS S3)
  • constant zero (Intel Cyclone V; Lattice iCE40)
  • fully configurable (Xilinx 7 Series)
  • init-matches-set (Lattice ECP5)
This means you can write a register init declaration which, when combined with an asynchronous set/reset wire, is impossible to directly satisfy in hardware. However, synchronous sets/resets can be emulated via input logic, so a global synchronous reset can always be implemented.
Fortunately, asynchronous resets to registers are rare, and not a good thing in general (they inhibits register retiming, which enables simpler, faster designs). So register initialization still works, except where you need an explicit asynchronous reset.

The "Last Assignment Wins" Reset Idiom

The common idiom for resets uses an if/else statement, where each register must be assigned a value in both clauses (else a latch may be synthesized), which means all registers must be reset, maximizing the size of the reset tree, which uses more routing and makes timing harder to meet. 

// Common idiom, which has a problem...
always @(posedge clock) begin
    if (reset == 1'b1) begin
        foo <= FOO_RESET;
        bar <= BAR_RESET; // but does not need a reset!
    end
    else begin
        foo <= foo_next;
        bar <= bar_next;
    end
end

If you must reset some registers, use one of the following idioms instead to minimize the size of the reset tree: 

// Ternary operator assignment for reset
always @(posedge clock) begin
    foo <= (reset == 1'b1) ? FOO_RESET : foo_next;
    bar <= bar_next
end

// Using "last assignment wins" semantics for reset
always @(posedge clock) begin
    foo <= foo_next;
    bar <= bar_next;

    if (reset == 1'b1) begin
        foo <= FOO_RESET;
    end
end

Credit for the "last assignment wins" idiom goes to Olof Kindgren (@olofkindgren): Resetting reset handling. This idiom also applies to VHDL.

Alse see this twitter thread which discusses a subtlety of "last assignment wins" which explains a subtlety of non-blocking assignments and ternary operators. Credit to Claire Wolf (@oe1cxw).

Combining Asynchronous Reset and Clock Enable

On FPGAs, the flip-flop reset hardware is asynchronous, and this causes difficulties when inferring flip-flops with clock_enables from Verilog code. The "last assignment wins" idiom to implement reset doesn't work here: having two separate if-statements (one for clock_enable followed by one for reset) does not work when the reset is asynchronously specified alongside the clock in the sensitivity list (as shown below), as there is no way to determine which signal in the sensitivity list each if statement should respond to.

Thus, correct flip-flop hardware inference depends on explicitly expressing the priority of the reset over the clock_enable structurally with nested if-statements, rather than implicitly through the Verilog event queue via the "last assignment wins" idiom.

This is very likely the *only* place you will ever need an asynchronous signal in a sensitivity list, or express explicit structural priority.

Also, even though the flip-flop reset hardware is asynchronous, it should be fed by a synchronous reset signal, otherwise if the flip-flop was not already at zero, it may flip to zero at a time such that the signal change reaches the next flip-flops down the line within their metastability window. 

`default_nettype none

module Register
#(
    parameter WORD_WIDTH  = 0,
    parameter RESET_VALUE = 0
)
(
    input   wire                        clock,
    input   wire                        clock_enable,
    input   wire                        areset,
    input   wire                        clear,
    input   wire    [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    data_in,
    output  reg     [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    data_out
);

    initial begin
        data_out = RESET_VALUE;
    end

    reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] selected = RESET_VALUE;

    always @(*) begin
        selected = (clear == 1'b1) ? RESET_VALUE : data_in;
    end

    always @(posedge clock or posedge areset) begin
        if (areset == 1'b1) begin
            data_out <= RESET_VALUE;
        end
        else begin
            if ((clock_enable == 1'b1) || (clear == 1'b1)) begin
                data_out <= selected;
            end
        end
    end

endmodule

Reset Sequencing

Various parts of a design may have to stay in reset for a minimum amount of time to properly initialize (e.g.: 200us for DDR2 RAM), and have to come out of reset in a certain order to avoid receiving undefined signals. You can sequence the resets by creating delayed copies of the power-on-reset with a few counters driven by the main clock, with the count values set as top-level design module parameters.

Some Corner Cases

Simulated Clock Generation

In simulation, a race condition can exist at time zero between the initial value assignment of a register and the first clock edge. For example: 

reg clock = 1'b0; // Counts as a negedge at time zero! (X -> 0)
reg foo   = 1'b0; // Also does X -> 0 at time zero.

// Simulate the clock
always begin
    #`HALF_PERIOD clock = ~clock;
end

// It is unclear if the clock edge or the "foo" initialization will happen first,
// so "bar" can get X for one simulation cycle...
always @(negedge clock) begin
    bar <= foo;
end

This race condition is another reason to only use @(posedge clock) in internal logic, but the same race condition will happen if the simulation clock happens to be initialized to 1'b1.

Instead, the following clock generation idiom (credit: Claire Wolf (@oe1cxw)) avoids the race condition by making use of undefined values and the identity operator (===) instead of the equality (==) operator: 

// NOTE: clock is left uninitialized, and thus X in most simulators, and will
// not trigger a (X -> 0) edge until after the simulated clock half-period delay.
always begin
    #`HALF_PERIOD clock = (clock === 1'b0);
end

The Simulation Clock module implements this idiom.

Constant Values

When a combinational procedural block contains only constant values on the right hand side, your linter and/or simulator may raise warnings. For example: 

reg foo;
always @(*) begin
    foo = 1'b0;
end

Since there can be no clock edge event or value change event of a right hand side value to trigger the procedural block, it is possible that the assignment never happens, depending on the interpretation of the CAD tool. This example is contrived, but such a situation will occur if you have a module output port which is both a register and outputs a constant. Since you cannot initialize a register in a port definition, you must instead set the constant value in an initial block, or at an extreme, change the reg port to a wire and assign it a constant value. (But this is strongly discouraged.) (This corner case was pointed out (and fixed!) by Rodrigo Melo.)

Avoiding Zero-Width Padding

When splitting a long bit vector into a number of words of a given width, or concatenating multiple words into a single wider vector, it's possible that you will need to pad a few unused bits, usually with zeros. Typically, you compute the width difference or the division remainder to get the pad width, then create the pad with a replication: 

localparam PAD_WIDTH   = BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH % WORD_WIDTH;
localparam TOTAL_WIDTH = WORD_WIDTH * WORD_COUNT;        // Alternate
localparam PAD_WIDTH   = BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH - TOTAL_WIDTH; // Alternate
localparam PAD         = {PAD_WIDTH{1'b0}};

However, this fails if no pad is needed. Variables of zero width cannot exist in Verilog, and a zero width replication is also illegal in Verilog-2001. Zero width concatenations are partially fixed in Verilog-2005, and thus SystemVerilog, but the above PAD would still fail at linting and elaboration since there are no other sized elements being concatenated.

We can eliminate zero from the possible PAD_WIDTH values by instead returning WORD_WIDTH if the pad width would have been zero. This works because a pad is necessarily between 0 and WORD_WIDTH-1 bits wide. We can then check later and instantiate a concatenation with or without a pad: 

reg [BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH-1:0] baz;
reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]       wibble, ...;

generate
    if (PAD_WIDTH != WORD_WIDTH) begin
        always @(*) begin
            baz = {PAD, wibble, ....}; // Padding MSBs here
        end
    end
    else begin
        always @(*) begin
            baz = {wibble, ... };
        end
    end
endgenerate

The Word Pad function implements this idiom, returning the pad width or `WORD_WIDTH` if the pad width would be zero.

Simple Cases for Zero-Padding

While the Word Pad function enables padding of arbitrary length at arbitrary positions (e.g.: you could pad in the middle of a wide vector with a special pattern instead of only zeros), the common case is to pad with zeros at the least-significant bits.

We can zero-pad the least significant bits of a wider vector automatically by making sure the vector is initialized to zero, then using a vector part select to place the concatenated words into the wider vector at just after the location of the pad. This approach automatically handles the case where the pad width equals zero without defining impossible zero-width variables or replications. 

localparam BIG_VECTOR_ZERO = {BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH{1'b0}};
localparam TOTAL_WIDTH     = WORD_WIDTH * WORD_COUNT;
localparam PAD_WIDTH       = BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH - TOTAL_WIDTH;

reg [BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH-1:0] baz = BIG_VECTOR_ZERO;
reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]       wibble, ...;

always @(*) begin
    baz [PAD_WIDTH +: TOTAL_WIDTH] = {wibble, ... };
end

Alternately, you could first concatenate the individual words into a vector of TOTAL_WIDTH bits, then pass it through a Width Adjuster to widen it to BIG_VECTOR_WIDTH, then pass that wider vector through a Bit Shifter to shift it left by PAD_WIDTH, filling in the least-significant bits with any pattern you wish.

1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 A Primer on Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) Theory 9 10 11 12

A Primer on Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) Theory

13 14

from FPGA Design Elements by Charles Eric LaForest, PhD., GateForge Consulting Ltd. 15 16

You can design plain synchronous logic driven by a single clock and be 17 certain it'll work (and you'd be right)...or you can design Clock Domain 18 Crossing (CDC) logic, and always be full of doubt. CDC logic is a 19 well-understood, but subtle design problem. There are many ways to get it 20 slightly wrong, causing intermittent failures, or ending up with a design that 21 complicates things and reduces performance. 22 23

Defining Metastability

24 25

Normally, digital logic works in a bistable mode: it's either in a high 26 state or low state, glossing over the fact that it takes time to move from one 27 state to the other. But so long as we sample states (e.g.: latch a signal) 28 far from those transitions, the assumption holds and everything 29 remains bistable. 30 31

A metastable state is simply any state that is neither high nor 32 low, but in between, like during a transition. And the closer to the mid-point 33 between states a metastable state is, the longer it takes to resolve to a valid 34 state and the less certainty there is which valid state that will be. So the 35 next logic output may remain metastable or resolve too late, a flip-flop may 36 sample the wrong state, and so on... and your system falls apart. 37 38

Metastability can happen at clock domain crossings since the sending clock 39 and receiving clock are (usually) asynchronous to eachother, or at least is 40 a lot simpler and robust to assume they are independent that way. Thus the 41 receiving clock might sample a signal from the sending clock domain too close 42 to its transitions, causing metastability. 43 44

(And it's impossible to design metastability-free circuits. That would 45 imply instantaneous state transitions, which isn't physically possible.) 46 47

The CDC Basic Building Block

48 49

A CDC Bit Synchronizer forms the core of 50 any CDC solution: a chain of two (or more) closely placed registers, with no 51 logic between them, and clocked by the receiving clock. The input register 52 receives a bit synchronized to a sending clock, and the output register sends a 53 version of that bit synchronized to the receiving clock. The duration between 54 bit transitions at the output may be of a different length than the original 55 bit, with a new duration of some multiple of the receiving clock period. 56 57

(You can find several more ready-to-use CDC modules here.) 58 59

Latency

60 61

Despite having two registers, a synchronizer has an unpredictable latency 62 from the point of view of the sending clock. A few different cases may happen, 63 which will also explain why a synchronizer is necessary: 64 65

    66 67
  1. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit just after a transition, such 68 that the output of the first synchronizer register updates nearly 69 simultaneously with the output of the register (in the sending clock domain) 70 feeding the synchronizer. The bit then exits the synchronizer on the next 71 receiving clock cycle. Latency: 1 cycle. 72
    • This is a corner case that happens only when the receiving clock edge 73 happens just after the sending clock edge and setup time was met. However, if 74 the clocks are very close in frequency and phase (plesiochronous), this corner 75 case can be the most frequent case!
    76 77
  2. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit properly some time after a 78 transition, which then exits the synchronizer on the next cycle. Latency: 2 79 cycles. 80 81
  3. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit just before a transition, so 82 that transition will pass through after two more cycles. Latency: 3 cycles. 83 84
  4. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit as it transitions, causing 85 the first register output to go metastable, which then settles into the correct 86 post-transition state and exits in the following cycle. Latency: 2 cycles. 87 88
  5. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit as it transitions, causing 89 the first register output to go metastable, which then settles into the 90 incorrect pre-transition state (no change). The post-transition bit is properly 91 captured in the next cycle, and exits in the cycle after that. Latency: 3 92 cycles. 93 94
  6. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit as it transitions, causing 95 the first register output to go metastable, which then settles into either the 96 pre-transition or post-transition state, but takes too long. The input 97 of the second register thus samples a metastable bit, causing one of the 98 previous two cases (4,5) to happen, or this case (6) again, at its own output. 99 However, this second metastability is exponentially less likely than 100 the first, and should in practice "never" happen (meaning, once every many, 101 many years on average, swamped by other failure modes). 102 103
104 105

Thus, depending on the alignment of the sending clock relative to the 106 receiving clock at that particular point in time, the output high and low 107 phases of a toggling bit may have different lengths, though the total period 108 remains the same. Also, the synchronizer prevents metastable state from 109 propagating into the rest of the logic, possibly causing an incorrect bit value 110 to be created. 111 112

The variable latency of a synchronizer implies an important design rule of 113 CDC: only one bit at a time may ever be synchronized across a given clock 114 domain crossing. If you tried to synchronize two bits in parallel, or the same 115 bit twice in parallel, there would be no guarantee that both bits would always 116 see the same latency through the synchronizer. With only a single bit, the 117 worst case is that its transition is missed, and will get captured in the next 118 receiving clock cycle. This delay causes no errors in itself, and only alters 119 the received bit duration by one receiving clock cycle. 120 121

Performance

122 123

How fast can we pass a bit through a synchronizer? The input bit needs to 124 remain stable long enough for the receiving clock to be always able to properly 125 capture the bit between two of its transitions (a cycle), else we could miss a 126 pulse. We can guarantee a proper capture if the minimum time between bit 127 transitions is equal or greater to 3 edges of the receiving clock, regardless 128 of clock alignment: 129 130

    131 132
  1. The 3 receiving clock edges are positive-negative-positive. Thus, if the 133 first posedge misses the first bit transition or goes metastable (see above), 134 the second one will capture the bit properly before the second transition. 135 136
  2. The 3 receiving clock edges are negative-positive-negative. Since the 137 posedge is squarely in the middle between the bit transitions, the bit is 138 properly sampled. 139 140
141 142

So, in the worst case of a bit which toggles each sending clock cycle, the 143 receiving clock must run at least 1.5x faster to provide 3 edges between bit 144 transitions. 145 146

This 1/3 sending/receiving ratio is another way to express the worst case 147 synchronizer latency of 3 cycles explained earlier: 3 receiving clock edges per 148 incoming bit transition means 6 edges total over a cycle (two transitions), 149 which necessarily contain 3 positive edges. 150 151

This 1/3 ratio is a stiff penalty, but peripherals clocks are usually slower 152 than core logic clocks, and the penalty can be spread out over multiple bits by 153 receiving multiple bits in one clock domain and then passing the resulting 154 whole word across clock domains (directly, without synchronization!), with a 155 single "valid" bit being synchronized across clock domains and acting as a 156 latch trigger in the receiving clock domain. 157 158

Special Cases: Extreme Clock Rate Differences and Logic Glitches

159 160

There is an implicit assumption in the above discussion about passing a bit 161 through a synchronizer: the receiving clock edges must coincide with the 162 incoming transition only once at most. Otherwise, in cases where the incoming 163 transition is very slow relative to the receiving clock, the receiving clock 164 will sample the incoming transition multiple times, which maximizes 165 the chance of a metastable event. This will manifest as multiple bit 166 transitions in the receiving clock domain. In these cases, the synchronizer 167 must be followed by a Debouncer. 168 169

For similar reasons, the input of a synchronizer must immediately come from 170 a register in the sending clock domain, without any logic before the 171 synchronizer. Otherwise, that final logic in the sending clock domain might 172 converge multiple paths of logic, and thus one or more glitches may appear at 173 its output due to different path or logic cell delays. Those glitches may get 174 sampled by the synchronizer and also result in multiple bit transitions in the 175 receiving clock domain. Again, if this case isn't avoidable, you must use a 176 Debouncer. 177 178

Further Issues and Reading

179 180

This article only scratches the surface of CDC, and glosses over accounting 181 for propagation delay, setup, and hold times when determining the maximum data 182 rate, or CAD tool issues regarding placement, timing analysis, and MTBF (Mean 183 Time Between Failure) calculations. Designing even simple CDC circuits requires 184 sweating a lot of details! 185 186

See Clifford E. Cummings' excellent paper 187 Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) Design & Verification 188 Techniques Using SystemVerilog for a larger and deeper overview of CDC. 189 190
