A Primer on Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) Theory

from FPGA Design Elements by Charles Eric LaForest, PhD., GateForge Consulting Ltd.

You can design plain synchronous logic driven by a single clock and be certain it'll work (and you'd be right)...or you can design Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) logic, and always be full of doubt. CDC logic is a well-understood, but subtle design problem. There are many ways to get it slightly wrong, causing intermittent failures, or ending up with a design that complicates things and reduces performance.

Defining Metastability

Normally, digital logic works in a bistable mode: it's either in a high state or low state, glossing over the fact that it takes time to move from one state to the other. But so long as we sample states (e.g.: latch a signal) far from those transitions, the assumption holds and everything remains bistable.

A metastable state is simply any state that is neither high nor low, but in between, like during a transition. And the closer to the mid-point between states a metastable state is, the longer it takes to resolve to a valid state and the less certainty there is which valid state that will be. So the next logic output may remain metastable or resolve too late, a flip-flop may sample the wrong state, and so on... and your system falls apart.

Metastability can happen at clock domain crossings since the sending clock and receiving clock are (usually) asynchronous to eachother, or at least is a lot simpler and robust to assume they are independent that way. Thus the receiving clock might sample a signal from the sending clock domain too close to its transitions, causing metastability.

(And it's impossible to design metastability-free circuits. That would imply instantaneous state transitions, which isn't physically possible.)

The CDC Basic Building Block

A CDC Bit Synchronizer forms the core of any CDC solution: a chain of two (or more) closely placed registers, with no logic between them, and clocked by the receiving clock. The input register receives a bit synchronized to a sending clock, and the output register sends a version of that bit synchronized to the receiving clock. The duration between bit transitions at the output may be of a different length than the original bit, with a new duration of some multiple of the receiving clock period.

(You can find several more ready-to-use CDC modules here.)

Latency

Despite having two registers, a synchronizer has an unpredictable latency from the point of view of the sending clock. A few different cases may happen, which will also explain why a synchronizer is necessary:

  1. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit just after a transition, such that the output of the first synchronizer register updates nearly simultaneously with the output of the register (in the sending clock domain) feeding the synchronizer. The bit then exits the synchronizer on the next receiving clock cycle. Latency: 1 cycle.
    • This is a corner case that happens only when the receiving clock edge happens just after the sending clock edge and setup time was met. However, if the clocks are very close in frequency and phase (plesiochronous), this corner case can be the most frequent case!
  2. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit properly some time after a transition, which then exits the synchronizer on the next cycle. Latency: 2 cycles.
  3. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit just before a transition, so that transition will pass through after two more cycles. Latency: 3 cycles.
  4. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit as it transitions, causing the first register output to go metastable, which then settles into the correct post-transition state and exits in the following cycle. Latency: 2 cycles.
  5. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit as it transitions, causing the first register output to go metastable, which then settles into the incorrect pre-transition state (no change). The post-transition bit is properly captured in the next cycle, and exits in the cycle after that. Latency: 3 cycles.
  6. The receiving clock captures the incoming bit as it transitions, causing the first register output to go metastable, which then settles into either the pre-transition or post-transition state, but takes too long. The input of the second register thus samples a metastable bit, causing one of the previous two cases (4,5) to happen, or this case (6) again, at its own output. However, this second metastability is exponentially less likely than the first, and should in practice "never" happen (meaning, once every many, many years on average, swamped by other failure modes).

Thus, depending on the alignment of the sending clock relative to the receiving clock at that particular point in time, the output high and low phases of a toggling bit may have different lengths, though the total period remains the same. Also, the synchronizer prevents metastable state from propagating into the rest of the logic, possibly causing an incorrect bit value to be created.

The variable latency of a synchronizer implies an important design rule of CDC: only one bit at a time may ever be synchronized across a given clock domain crossing. If you tried to synchronize two bits in parallel, or the same bit twice in parallel, there would be no guarantee that both bits would always see the same latency through the synchronizer. With only a single bit, the worst case is that its transition is missed, and will get captured in the next receiving clock cycle. This delay causes no errors in itself, and only alters the received bit duration by one receiving clock cycle.

Performance

How fast can we pass a bit through a synchronizer? The input bit needs to remain stable long enough for the receiving clock to be always able to properly capture the bit between two of its transitions (a cycle), else we could miss a pulse. We can guarantee a proper capture if the minimum time between bit transitions is equal or greater to 3 edges of the receiving clock, regardless of clock alignment:

  1. The 3 receiving clock edges are positive-negative-positive. Thus, if the first posedge misses the first bit transition or goes metastable (see above), the second one will capture the bit properly before the second transition.
  2. The 3 receiving clock edges are negative-positive-negative. Since the posedge is squarely in the middle between the bit transitions, the bit is properly sampled.

So, in the worst case of a bit which toggles each sending clock cycle, the receiving clock must run at least 1.5x faster to provide 3 edges between bit transitions.

This 1/3 sending/receiving ratio is another way to express the worst case synchronizer latency of 3 cycles explained earlier: 3 receiving clock edges per incoming bit transition means 6 edges total over a cycle (two transitions), which necessarily contain 3 positive edges.

This 1/3 ratio is a stiff penalty, but peripherals clocks are usually slower than core logic clocks, and the penalty can be spread out over multiple bits by receiving multiple bits in one clock domain and then passing the resulting whole word across clock domains (directly, without synchronization!), with a single "valid" bit being synchronized across clock domains and acting as a latch trigger in the receiving clock domain.

Special Cases: Extreme Clock Rate Differences and Logic Glitches

There is an implicit assumption in the above discussion about passing a bit through a synchronizer: the receiving clock edges must coincide with the incoming transition only once at most. Otherwise, in cases where the incoming transition is very slow relative to the receiving clock, the receiving clock will sample the incoming transition multiple times, which maximizes the chance of a metastable event. This will manifest as multiple bit transitions in the receiving clock domain. In these cases, the synchronizer must be followed by a Debouncer.

For similar reasons, the input of a synchronizer must immediately come from a register in the sending clock domain, without any logic before the synchronizer. Otherwise, that final logic in the sending clock domain might converge multiple paths of logic, and thus one or more glitches may appear at its output due to different path or logic cell delays. Those glitches may get sampled by the synchronizer and also result in multiple bit transitions in the receiving clock domain. Again, if this case isn't avoidable, you must use a Debouncer.

Further Issues and Reading

This article only scratches the surface of CDC, and glosses over accounting for propagation delay, setup, and hold times when determining the maximum data rate, or CAD tool issues regarding placement, timing analysis, and MTBF (Mean Time Between Failure) calculations. 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9 On The Implementation of Finite State Machines 10 11 12 13

On The Implementation of Finite State Machines

14 15

With FSMs, the designer can statefully control data processing logic and 16 thus implement arbitrary functions up to a full CPU. FSMs can be implemented 17 with design elements using localparams (for named constants and states), 18 register modules (for outputs and holding state), comparison modules (eq, lt, 19 gt, neq), multiplexer modules (to select the next state), and wires to name 20 signals and tie everything together. 21 22

Once the designer has defined the state transition diagram, they can 23 build-up the design of the FSM incrementally using design elements by: 24 25

    26
  1. defining any constraints on outputs (e.g. only asserted in certain states) 27
  2. defining the basic operations on the datapath, as Boolean composition of the control inputs and outputs 28
  3. defining the transformations on the datapath (what do certain operations means when in given states) 29
  4. defining the state transitions (given a certain transformation, what is the next state) 30
  5. defining the control outputs (which transformations assert a given output, as simple Boolean logic on the transformations) 31
32 33

In the state transition diagram, each node and its edges are designed as a 34 "checker" module which takes the current state and some inputs, and if they are 35 equal to the expected values, raises a "match" output. This "match" output is 36 then used to set the next state. There are three ways to compose these 37 "checker" modules to calculate the nest state: 38 39

    40 41
  1. A chain of multiplexers which either pass along the current state or 42 previously selected state, or replace that with a new selected state. This 43 design is easy to follow, but depends on the tool's logic optimizer to combine 44 terms in the chain of logic. log2N bits are required for N states. 45 46
  2. A parallel tree of multiplexers outputting either a chosen next state or 47 zero, plus one extra bit of logic which outputs either zero or the current 48 state if no other multiplexer is outputting a chosen next state. All the 49 multiplexer outputs are OR-reduced to the final next state. This design has a 50 shorter critical path, but requires a bit more manual design effort, and 51 converges a lot of wasted calculations (the outputs of checkers which could 52 never be active in the current state) into a large OR gate, which suggests a 53 more efficient alternative. log2N bits are required for N states. 54 55
  3. Separate parallel "checkers" whose output sets or clears a single 56 flip-flop. Each flip-flop represents a state. The FSM is in a given state if 57 one and only one flip-flop is set (one-hot encoding). This design is the most 58 parallel, has the shortest logic path, and may support pipelined operation or 59 even overlapping states given suitable state encodings. N bits are required 60 for N states. 61 62
63 64

There's an apparent problem where a chain of multiplexers imposes a priority 65 order on "match" outputs, causing some concurrent state transitions to be 66 missed, or a tree of multiplexers funneling to a final OR gate before the state 67 register could merge multiple set "match" outputs into nonsense state values, 68 or multiple bits could become set in an otherwise one-hot encoding. However, 69 these cases are impossible for DFAs (Deterministic Finite Automata), where 70 identical combinations of current state and current signals cannot lead to 71 multiple, different states. To create such a condition in the above 72 constraints/operations/transformations/transitions/control (COTTC) scheme, we 73 would have to explicitly write multiple checkers which test for the same 74 transformation but select different next states. Although logically allowable, 75 the conflict will be visible to the user. 76 77

Back to FPGA Design Elements 78 79 80

file.content:
On The Implementation of Finite State Machines

With FSMs, the designer can statefully control data processing logic and thus implement arbitrary functions up to a full CPU. FSMs can be implemented with design elements using localparams (for named constants and states), register modules (for outputs and holding state), comparison modules (eq, lt, gt, neq), multiplexer modules (to select the next state), and wires to name signals and tie everything together.

Once the designer has defined the state transition diagram, they can build-up the design of the FSM incrementally using design elements by:

  1. defining any constraints on outputs (e.g. only asserted in certain states)
  2. defining the basic operations on the datapath, as Boolean composition of the control inputs and outputs
  3. defining the transformations on the datapath (what do certain operations means when in given states)
  4. defining the state transitions (given a certain transformation, what is the next state)
  5. defining the control outputs (which transformations assert a given output, as simple Boolean logic on the transformations)

In the state transition diagram, each node and its edges are designed as a "checker" module which takes the current state and some inputs, and if they are equal to the expected values, raises a "match" output. This "match" output is then used to set the next state. There are three ways to compose these "checker" modules to calculate the nest state:

  1. A chain of multiplexers which either pass along the current state or previously selected state, or replace that with a new selected state. This design is easy to follow, but depends on the tool's logic optimizer to combine terms in the chain of logic. log2N bits are required for N states.
  2. A parallel tree of multiplexers outputting either a chosen next state or zero, plus one extra bit of logic which outputs either zero or the current state if no other multiplexer is outputting a chosen next state. All the multiplexer outputs are OR-reduced to the final next state. This design has a shorter critical path, but requires a bit more manual design effort, and converges a lot of wasted calculations (the outputs of checkers which could never be active in the current state) into a large OR gate, which suggests a more efficient alternative. log2N bits are required for N states.
  3. Separate parallel "checkers" whose output sets or clears a single flip-flop. Each flip-flop represents a state. The FSM is in a given state if one and only one flip-flop is set (one-hot encoding). This design is the most parallel, has the shortest logic path, and may support pipelined operation or even overlapping states given suitable state encodings. N bits are required for N states.

There's an apparent problem where a chain of multiplexers imposes a priority order on "match" outputs, causing some concurrent state transitions to be missed, or a tree of multiplexers funneling to a final OR gate before the state register could merge multiple set "match" outputs into nonsense state values, or multiple bits could become set in an otherwise one-hot encoding. However, these cases are impossible for DFAs (Deterministic Finite Automata), where identical combinations of current state and current signals cannot lead to multiple, different states. To create such a condition in the above constraints/operations/transformations/transitions/control (COTTC) scheme, we would have to explicitly write multiple checkers which test for the same transformation but select different next states. Although logically allowable, the conflict will be visible to the user.

Back to FPGA Design Elements

1 #! /usr/bin/python3 2 3 """ 4 This program converts Verilog code to a basic webpage: 5 6 Lines that *begin* with a line comment ("// ..."), or groups of such lines 7 separated by blank lines, get put together as a block of text and processed as 8 Markdown text. 9 10 The remaining non-blank, non-line-comment lines are code, which gets wrapped in 11 a 
 block, and any module name where a converted Verilog to HTML file
12	exists gets transformed into a link. This depends on the convention of one
13	module per file where the filename matches the module name.
14	
15	Thus you can keep the Verilog code, its documentation, and its presentation in sync.
16	
17	A file is only updated if the newly generated HTML is not identical to the existing HTML file.
18	
19	Copyright (c) 2019-2024 Charles Eric LaForest, PhD
20	License: https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT
21	"""
22	
23	import markdown
24	import sys
25	from os.path import isfile
26	
27	# Web page header and footer, with a placeholder for the page title.
28	
29	header = """
30	
31	
32	
33	
34	
35	
36	{1}
37	
38	
39	
40	
Source

41	
License

42	
Index

43	
44	"""
45	
46	footer = """

47	
Back to FPGA Design Elements
48	
fpgacpu.ca

49	
50	
51	
52	"""
53	
54	def base_filename(filename):
55	    """Strip Verilog filename extensions"""
56	    return filename.replace(".vh", "").replace(".v", "")
57	
58	def output_filename(filename):
59	    """Convert input Verilog filename to output HTML filename"""
60	    return base_filename(filename) + ".html"
61	
62	def filename_to_title(filename):
63	    """Convert the Verilog filename into the web page title"""
64	    return base_filename(filename).replace("_", " ")
65	
66	def is_verilog_filename(filename):
67	    """This prevents accidentally processing other files of the same prefix name"""
68	    return verilog_filename[-2:] == ".v" or verilog_filename[-3:] == ".vh"
69	
70	def is_comment(line):
71	    return line.startswith("//")
72	
73	def is_header(line):
74	    return line.startswith("//#")
75	
76	def is_blank(line):
77	    """If a line is only spaces or tabs and a return, it's blank."""
78	    return line.strip(" \t") == "\n"
79	
80	def is_eof(line):
81	    """readline only ever returns an empty line at EOF"""
82	    return line == ""
83	
84	def process_first_paragraph(line, f):
85	    """Extract just the first paragraph of comments after header"""
86	    paragraph_block = ""
87	    while (is_header(line) or is_blank(line)) and not is_eof(line):
88	        line = f.readline()
89	    while is_comment(line) and not is_blank(line) and not is_eof(line):
90	        line = line.lstrip("/")
91	        paragraph_block = paragraph_block + line
92	        line = f.readline()
93	    paragraph_block = paragraph_block.replace("\n", "").strip()
94	    f.seek(0)
95	    return line, paragraph_block

#! /usr/bin/python3 """ This program converts Verilog code to a basic webpage: Lines that *begin* with a line comment ("// ..."), or groups of such lines separated by blank lines, get put together as a block of text and processed as Markdown text. The remaining non-blank, non-line-comment lines are code, which gets wrapped in a 
 block, and any module name where a converted Verilog to HTML file
exists gets transformed into a link. This depends on the convention of one
module per file where the filename matches the module name.

Thus you can keep the Verilog code, its documentation, and its presentation in sync.

A file is only updated if the newly generated HTML is not identical to the existing HTML file.

Copyright (c) 2019-2024 Charles Eric LaForest, PhD
License: https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT
"""

import markdown
import sys
from os.path import isfile

# Web page header and footer, with a placeholder for the page title.

header = """






{1}




Source


License


Index


"""

footer = """


Back to FPGA Design Elements

fpgacpu.ca




"""

def base_filename(filename):
    """Strip Verilog filename extensions"""
    return filename.replace(".vh", "").replace(".v", "")

def output_filename(filename):
    """Convert input Verilog filename to output HTML filename"""
    return base_filename(filename) + ".html"

def filename_to_title(filename):
    """Convert the Verilog filename into the web page title"""
    return base_filename(filename).replace("_", " ")

def is_verilog_filename(filename):
    """This prevents accidentally processing other files of the same prefix name"""
    return verilog_filename[-2:] == ".v" or verilog_filename[-3:] == ".vh"

def is_comment(line):
    return line.startswith("//")

def is_header(line):
    return line.startswith("//#")

def is_blank(line):
    """If a line is only spaces or tabs and a return, it's blank."""
    return line.strip(" \t") == "\n"

def is_eof(line):
    """readline only ever returns an empty line at EOF"""
    return line == ""

def process_first_paragraph(line, f):
    """Extract just the first paragraph of comments after header"""
    paragraph_block = ""
    while (is_header(line) or is_blank(line)) and not is_eof(line):
        line = f.readline()
    while is_comment(line) and not is_blank(line) and not is_eof(line):
        line = line.lstrip("/")
        paragraph_block = paragraph_block + line
        line = f.readline()
    paragraph_block = paragraph_block.replace("\n", "").strip()
    f.seek(0)
    return line, paragraph_block

def process_comments(line, processed_contents, f):
    """Take in comment/blank lines until a line is neither.
       Then process them as Markdown and output the XHTML."""
    comment_block = ""
    while (is_comment(line) or is_blank(line)) and not is_eof(line):
        comment_block = comment_block + line.lstrip("/")
        line = f.readline()
    html = markdown.markdown(comment_block)
    processed_contents += html + "\n\n"
    return line, processed_contents

def add_file_links(line):
    """Checks each word in a line. 
       If an HTML file exists with that word as a name, replace it with a link.
       This will transform module instance names to links.
       This depends on the convention of one module per file
       where the filename matches the module name."""
    split_line = line.strip().split()
    for word in split_line:
        # Remove extra characters to recognize include files
        word = word.replace('"','').replace(".vh","")
        filename = "./" + word + ".html"
        link = """{1}""".format(filename, word)
        if isfile(filename):
            line = line.replace(word, link)
    return line

def process_code(line, processed_contents, f):
    """Take in code lines until a line is a comment.
       Then remove the previous line if it's blank.
       Then wrap the code in a 
 block."""
    code_block = []
    while not is_comment(line) and not is_eof(line):
        code_block.append(line)
        line = f.readline()
    if is_blank(code_block[-1]):
        code_block.pop()
    processed_contents += "
\n"
    for code_line in code_block:
        code_line = add_file_links(code_line)
        processed_contents += code_line
    processed_contents += "
\n\n"
    return line, processed_contents

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Skip argv[0] (the script name)
    for verilog_filename in sys.argv[1:]:
        if not is_verilog_filename(verilog_filename):
            continue
        html_filename = output_filename(verilog_filename)

        try:
            f = open(html_filename, 'r')
            existing_file_contents = f.read()
            f.close()
        except OSError:
            existing_file_contents = ""
            pass

        f = open(verilog_filename, 'r')
        processed_contents = ""
        line = f.readline()
        line, meta_description = process_first_paragraph(line, f)
        title = filename_to_title(verilog_filename)
        processed_contents += header.format(meta_description, title, verilog_filename, html_filename)
        # Note the returning of the last line read and restarting of parsing.
        # This avoids the need for look-ahead to know when to end a comment or code block.
        while not is_eof(line):
            if is_comment(line):
                line, processed_contents = process_comments(line, processed_contents, f)
                continue
            if not is_comment(line) and not is_blank(line):
                line, processed_contents = process_code(line, processed_contents, f)
                continue
            line = f.readline()
        processed_contents += footer
        f.close()

        if processed_contents != existing_file_contents:
            print("Updating {0}".format(html_filename));
            f = open(html_filename, 'w')
            f.write(processed_contents)
            f.close()
        #else:
        #    print("Skipping {0}".format(html_filename));

    print("Done.")

1	
2	//# A Synchronous Register to Store and Control Data
3	
4	// It may seem silly to implement a register module rather than let the HDL
5	// infer it, but doing so separates data and control at the most basic level,
6	// including various kinds of resets, which are part of control. This
7	// separation of data and control allows us to simplify the control logic and
8	// reduce the need for some routing resources.
9	
10	//## Power-on-Reset
11	//
12	// On FPGAs, the initial state of registers is set in the configuration
13	// bitstream and applied by special power-on reset circuitry. The initial
14	// state of a design is available "for free" *and can be returned to at
15	// run-time*, which removes the need for that control and data logic.
16	
17	//## Asynchronous Reset
18	
19	// The asynchronous reset is not implemented here as its existence prevents
20	// register retiming, even if tied to zero. This limitation complicates design
21	// and reduces performance as we would have to manually place registers to
22	// properly pipeline logic. If you absolutely need an asynchronous reset for
23	// ASIC implementation or for some critical registers, use the
24	// [Register_areset](./Register_areset.html) instead.
25	
26	//## Synchronous Reset (a.k.a. Clear)
27	
28	// If you need to clear the register during normal operation, use the
29	// synchronous clear input. This may create extra logic, but that logic gets
30	// folded into other logic feeding data to the register, and would have been
31	// necessary anyway but present as another case in the surrounding logic.
32	// Having a clear input allows us to get to the initial power-on-reset state
33	// without complicating the design.
34	
35	//## Implementation
36	
37	// Let's begin with the usual front matter:
38	
39	`default_nettype none
40	
41	module Register
42	#(
43	    parameter WORD_WIDTH  = 0,
44	    parameter RESET_VALUE = 0
45	)
46	(
47	    input   wire                        clock,
48	    input   wire                        clock_enable,
49	    input   wire                        clear,
50	    input   wire    [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    data_in,
51	    output  reg     [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    data_out
52	);
53	
54	    initial begin
55	        data_out = RESET_VALUE;
56	    end
57	
58	// Here, we use the  "last assignment wins" idiom (See
59	// [Resets](./verilog.html#resets)) to implement reset.  This is also one
60	// place where we cannot use ternary operators, else the last assignment for
61	// clear (e.g.: `data_out <= (clear == 1'b1) ? RESET_VALUE : data_out;`) would
62	// override any previous assignment with the current value of `data_out` if
63	// `clear` is not asserted!
64	
65	    always @(posedge clock) begin
66	        if (clock_enable == 1'b1) begin
67	            data_out <= data_in;
68	        end
69	
70	        if (clear == 1'b1) begin
71	            data_out <= RESET_VALUE;
72	        end
73	    end
74	
75	endmodule
76	
77

1 2 //# Pipeline Skid Buffer 3 4 // Decouples two sides of a ready/valid handshake to allow back-to-back 5 // transfers without a combinational path between input and output, thus 6 // pipelining the path. *Can function as a two-entry Circular Buffer.* 7 8 // A skid buffer is the smallest [Pipeline FIFO 9 // Buffer](./Pipeline_FIFO_Buffer.html), with only two entries. It is useful 10 // when you need to pipeline the path between a sender and a receiver for 11 // concurrency and/or timing, but not to smooth-out data rate mismatches. It 12 // also only requires two data registers, which at this scale is smaller than 13 // LUTRAMs or Block RAMs (depending on implementation), and has more freedom 14 // of placement and routing. 15 16 // Alternately, a Skid Buffer is also known as a Carloni Buffer. For reference, 17 // see Abbas and Betz ["Latency Insensitive Design Styles for 18 // FPGAs"](./reading.html#elastic) (FPL, 2018). 19 20 //## Background 21 22 // Networks-on-Chip (NoC) and elastic pipelines have as a fundamental building 23 // block a handshaking mechanism where each end of a link can signal if they 24 // have data to send ("valid"), or if they are able to receive data ("ready"). 25 // When both ends agree (valid and ready both high), a data transfer occurs on 26 // that clock cycle. 27 28 // However, pipelining handshaking is more complicated: simply adding 29 // a pipeline register to the valid, ready, and data lines will work, but now 30 // each transfer take two cycles to start, and two cycles to stop. This isn't 31 // bad in terms of bandwidth if you know you can transfer a block of data per 32 // handshake, but now the receiver has to be aware of how many pipeline stages 33 // exist between it and the sender, and thus must have sufficient internal 34 // buffering to absorb the data that keeps arriving after it signals it is no 35 // longer ready to receive more data. 36 37 // This is the basis of credit-based connections (which I'm not getting into 38 // here), which maximize bandwidth over long pipelines, but are overkill if you 39 // simply need to add a single pipeline stage between two ends, without having to 40 // modify them, so as to meet timing or allow each end to send off one item of 41 // data without having to wait for a response (thus overlapping communication and 42 // computation, which is desirable). 43 44 // This fundamental pipeline block is the *skid buffer*. 45 46 //## Figuring Out The Requirements 47 48 // To begin designing a skid buffer, let's imagine a single unit which can 49 // perform a valid/ready handshake and receive an input item of data, then 50 // performs the same handshake with the other end to output the data. 51 52 // Input Output 53 // ----- ------ 54 // ------------- 55 // ready <--| |<-- ready 56 // valid -->| Skid Buffer |--> valid 57 // data -->| |--> data 58 // ------------- 59 60 // Ideally, the input and output interfaces operate concurrently for maximum 61 // bandwidth: in the same clock cycle, a new data item is received on the 62 // input interface and put into a register, and that same register is 63 // simultaneously read out by the output interface. However, if the output 64 // interface is not transfering data on a given cycle, the input interface 65 // must not transfer data during that cycle also, else we will overwrite the 66 // data register before it was read out. To avoid this problem, the input 67 // interface should declare itself not ready in the same cycle as the output 68 // interface declaring itself not ready. But this forms a direct combinational 69 // connection between them, not a pipelined one. *If we could connect both 70 // interfaces directly, and not affect timing or concurrency, we wouldn't need 71 // pipelining in the first place!* 72 73 // To resolve this contradiction, we need an extra buffer register to capture 74 // the incoming data during a clock cycle where the input interface is 75 // transferring data, but the output interface isn't, and there is already 76 // data in the main register. Then, in the next cycle, the input interface can 77 // signal it is no longer ready, and no data gets lost. We can imagine this 78 // extra buffer register as allowing the input interface to "skid" to a stop, 79 // rather than stopping immediately, which we'd previously found contradicts 80 // our pipelining requirements. 81 82 //## Circular Buffer Mode 83 84 // Normally, a Skid Buffer reads in one value and read out one value each 85 // cycle. Should there be a stall at the output, the Skid Buffer fills up 86 // after one more input handshake and will not complete another input 87 // handshake until a value has been read out, causing a one-cycle stall at the 88 // input. You can think of this as buffering the *earliest* values from the 89 // pipeline. 90 91 // Setting `CIRCULAR_BUFFER` parameter to a non-zero value changes the 92 // behaviour at the input: the input handshake can always complete, discarding 93 // the earlier data already at the buffer output even if it is never read out 94 // and replacing it with the next previously buffered value. You can think of 95 // this as buffering the *latest* values from the pipeline. This is 96 // a two-entry circular buffer. 97 98 // Contrary to normal operation, simultaneous input and ouput handshakes are 99 // possible on a full Skid Buffer in Circular Buffer Mode, giving full 100 // throughput with 2 cycles of latency. This is possible since `input_ready` 101 // no longer depends on the empty/full state of the buffer (which forces 102 // alternation of input and output handshakes), nor on the state of the output 103 // handshake (which is disallowed to prevent creating a combinational path 104 // between input and output). 105 106 `default_nettype none 107 108 module Pipeline_Skid_Buffer 109 #( 110 parameter WORD_WIDTH = 0, 111 parameter CIRCULAR_BUFFER = 0 // non-zero to enable 112 ) 113 ( 114 input wire clock, 115 input wire clear, 116 117 input wire input_valid, 118 output wire input_ready, 119 input wire [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] input_data, 120 121 output wire output_valid, 122 input wire output_ready, 123 output wire [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] output_data 124 ); 125 126 localparam WORD_ZERO = {WORD_WIDTH{1'b0}}; 127 128 //## Data Path 129 130 // Feed the data_out register either from the input_data, or a buffered copy of 131 // input_data. Write to registers only if enabled by control. 132 133 // Funneling into a single data_out register rather than selecting between two 134 // equal output registers avoids a mux after registers, fed by two data 135 // streams (thus more routing and delay). A single output register also 136 // retimes more easily into downstream logic. 137 138 // Set up the default control values to match the "empty" state of the skid 139 // buffer, so the first input_data to arrive ends up in the data_out by 140 // default. We don't have to worry about state here, just pass the data 141 // through unless told otherwise. 142 143 reg data_buffer_wren = 1'b0; // EMPTY at start, so don't load. 144 wire [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] data_buffer_out; 145 146 Register 147 #( 148 .WORD_WIDTH (WORD_WIDTH), 149 .RESET_VALUE (WORD_ZERO) 150 ) 151 data_buffer_reg 152 ( 153 .clock (clock), 154 .clock_enable (data_buffer_wren), 155 .clear (clear), 156 .data_in (input_data), 157 .data_out (data_buffer_out) 158 ); 159 160 reg data_out_wren = 1'b1; // EMPTY at start, so accept data. 161 reg use_buffered_data = 1'b0; 162 reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] selected_data = WORD_ZERO; 163 164 always @(*) begin 165 selected_data = (use_buffered_data == 1'b1) ? data_buffer_out : input_data; 166 end 167 168 Register 169 #( 170 .WORD_WIDTH (WORD_WIDTH), 171 .RESET_VALUE (WORD_ZERO) 172 ) 173 data_out_reg 174 ( 175 .clock (clock), 176 .clock_enable (data_out_wren), 177 .clear (clear), 178 .data_in (selected_data), 179 .data_out (output_data) 180 ); 181 182 //## Control Path 183 184 // We separate the control path so the associated data path does not have to 185 // know anything about the current state or its encoding. 186 187 // This FSM assumes the usual meaning and behaviour of valid/ready handshake 188 // signals: when both are high, data transfers at the end of the clock cycle. 189 // It is an error to raise ready when not able to accept data (thus losing the 190 // incoming data), or to raise valid when not able to send data (thus 191 // duplicating previously sent data). *These error situations are not 192 // handled.* 193 194 // To operate our datapath as a skid buffer, we need to understand which 195 // states we want to allow it to be in, and which state transitions we also 196 // allow. This skid buffer has three states: 197 198 // 1. It is Empty. 199 // 2. It is Busy, holding one item of data in the main register, either waiting 200 // or actively transferring data through that register. 201 // 3. It is Full, holding data in both registers, and stopped until the main 202 // register is emptied and simultaneously refilled from the buffer register, so no 203 // data is lost or reordered. (Without an available empty register, the 204 // input interface cannot skid to a stop, so it must signal it is not ready.) 205 // 4. It is Full and in Circular Buffer Mode, holding data in both registers, 206 // and can accept new data into the buffer register while simultaneously 207 // replacing the contents of the main register with the current contents of 208 // the buffer register. 209 210 // The operations which transition between these states are: 211 212 // * the input interface inserting a data item into the datapath (`+`) 213 // * the output interface removing a data item from the datapath (`-`) 214 // * both interfaces inserting and removing at the same time (`+-`) 215 216 // We also descriptively name each transition between states. These names will 217 // show up later in the code. 218 219 //
220	//                 /--\ +- flow
221	//                 |  |
222	//          load   |  v   fill
223	// -------   +    ------   +    ------        (CBM)
224	//|       | ---> |      | ---> |      | ---\ +  dump
225	//| Empty |      | Busy |      | Full |    |   or
226	//|       | <--- |      | <--- |      | <--/ +- pass
227	// -------    -   ------    -   ------
228	//         unload         flush
229	//
230 231 // We can see from the resulting state diagram that when the datapath is empty, 232 // it can only support an insertion, and when it is full, it can only support 233 // a removal, unles in Circular Buffer Mode (CBM) where it can support 234 // insertion and removal when full. *These constraints will become very 235 // important later on.* Normally, if the interfaces try to remove while Empty, 236 // or insert while Full, data will be duplicated or lost, respectively. 237 238 // This simple FSM description helped us clarify the problem, but it also 239 // glossed over the potential complexity of the implementation: 3 states, each 240 // connected to 2 signals (valid/ready) per interface, for a total of 16 241 // possible transitions out of each state, or 48 possible state transitions 242 // total. The Circular Buffer Mode does not introduce a new state, as it is 243 // an elaboration-time parameter, not a run-time input. 244 245 // We don't want to have to manually enumerate all the transitions to then 246 // coalesce the equivalent ones and rule out all the impossible or illegal 247 // ones. Instead, if we express in logic the constraints on removals and 248 // insertions we determined from the state diagram, and the possible 249 // transformations on the datapath, we then get the state transition logic and 250 // datapath control signal logic almost for free. 251 252 // Lets describe the possible states of the datapath, and initialize it. This 253 // code describes a binary state encoding, but the CAD tool can re-encode and 254 // re-number the state encoding. Usually this is beneficial, but if the 255 // states+inputs fit in a single LUT, forcing binary encoding reduces area. 256 // See what works best (i.e.: reaches the highest speed) for your given FPGA. 257 258 localparam STATE_BITS = 2; 259 260 localparam [STATE_BITS-1:0] EMPTY = 'd0; // Output and buffer registers empty 261 localparam [STATE_BITS-1:0] BUSY = 'd1; // Output register holds data 262 localparam [STATE_BITS-1:0] FULL = 'd2; // Both output and buffer registers hold data 263 // There is no case where only the buffer register would hold data. 264 265 // No handling of erroneous and unreachable state 3. 266 // We could check and raise an error flag. 267 268 wire [STATE_BITS-1:0] state; 269 reg [STATE_BITS-1:0] state_next = EMPTY; 270 271 // Now, let's express the constraints we figured out from the state diagram: 272 273 // * The input interface can only insert when the datapath is not full. 274 // * The output interface can only remove data when the datapath is not empty, 275 // except in Circular Buffer Mode, where it can also insert. 276 277 // We do this by computing the allowable output read/valid handshake signals 278 // based on the datapath state. We use `state_next` so we can have nice 279 // registered outputs. This little bit of code prunes away a large number of 280 // invalid state transitions. If some other logic seems to be missing, first 281 // see if this code has made it unnecessary. 282 283 // *This tiny bit of code is critical* since it also implies the fundamental 284 // operating assumptions of a skid buffer: that one interface cannot have its 285 // current state depend on the current state of the other interface, as that 286 // would be a combinational path between both interfaces. 287 288 // Compute `ready` for the input interface. In Circular Buffer Mode, the input 289 // interface is always ready. 290 291 Register 292 #( 293 .WORD_WIDTH (1), 294 .RESET_VALUE (1'b1) // EMPTY at start, so accept data 295 ) 296 input_ready_reg 297 ( 298 .clock (clock), 299 .clock_enable (1'b1), 300 .clear (clear), 301 .data_in ((state_next != FULL) || (CIRCULAR_BUFFER != 0)), 302 .data_out (input_ready) 303 ); 304 305 // Compute `valid` for the output interface 306 307 Register 308 #( 309 .WORD_WIDTH (1), 310 .RESET_VALUE (1'b0) 311 ) 312 output_valid_reg 313 ( 314 .clock (clock), 315 .clock_enable (1'b1), 316 .clear (clear), 317 .data_in (state_next != EMPTY), 318 .data_out (output_valid) 319 ); 320 321 // After, let's describe the interface signal conditions which implement our 322 // two basic operations on the datapath: insert and remove. This also weeds 323 // out a number of possible state transitions. 324 325 reg insert = 1'b0; 326 reg remove = 1'b0; 327 328 always @(*) begin 329 insert = (input_valid == 1'b1) && (input_ready == 1'b1); 330 remove = (output_valid == 1'b1) && (output_ready == 1'b1); 331 end 332 333 // Now that we have our datapath states and operations, let's use them to 334 // describe the possible transformations to the datapath, and in which state 335 // they can happen. You'll see that these exactly describe each of the 336 // 5 edges in the state diagram (7 in Circular Buffer Mode), and since we've 337 // pruned the space of possible interface conditions, we only need the minimum 338 // logic to describe them, and this logic gets re-used a lot later on, 339 // simplifying the code. 340 341 342 reg load = 1'b0; // Empty datapath inserts data into output register. 343 reg flow = 1'b0; // New inserted data into output register as the old data is removed. 344 reg fill = 1'b0; // New inserted data into buffer register. Data not removed from output register. 345 reg flush = 1'b0; // Move data from buffer register into output register. Remove old data. No new data inserted. 346 reg unload = 1'b0; // Remove data from output register, leaving the datapath empty. 347 reg dump = 1'b0; // New inserted data into buffer register. Move data from buffer register into output register. Discard old output data. (CBM) 348 reg pass = 1'b0; // New inserted data into buffer register. Move data from buffer register into output register. Remove old output data. (CBM) 349 350 always @(*) begin 351 load = (state == EMPTY) && (insert == 1'b1) && (remove == 1'b0); 352 flow = (state == BUSY) && (insert == 1'b1) && (remove == 1'b1); 353 fill = (state == BUSY) && (insert == 1'b1) && (remove == 1'b0); 354 unload = (state == BUSY) && (insert == 1'b0) && (remove == 1'b1); 355 flush = (state == FULL) && (insert == 1'b0) && (remove == 1'b1); 356 dump = (state == FULL) && (insert == 1'b1) && (remove == 1'b0) && (CIRCULAR_BUFFER != 0); 357 pass = (state == FULL) && (insert == 1'b1) && (remove == 1'b1) && (CIRCULAR_BUFFER != 0); 358 end 359 360 // And now we simply need to calculate the next state after each datapath 361 // transformations: 362 363 always @(*) begin 364 state_next = (load == 1'b1) ? BUSY : state; 365 state_next = (flow == 1'b1) ? BUSY : state_next; 366 state_next = (fill == 1'b1) ? FULL : state_next; 367 state_next = (flush == 1'b1) ? BUSY : state_next; 368 state_next = (unload == 1'b1) ? EMPTY : state_next; 369 state_next = (dump == 1'b1) ? FULL : state_next; 370 state_next = (pass == 1'b1) ? FULL : state_next; 371 end 372 373 Register 374 #( 375 .WORD_WIDTH (STATE_BITS), 376 .RESET_VALUE (EMPTY) // Initial state 377 ) 378 state_reg 379 ( 380 .clock (clock), 381 .clock_enable (1'b1), 382 .clear (clear), 383 .data_in (state_next), 384 .data_out (state) 385 ); 386 387 // Similarly, from the datapath transformations, we can compute the necessary 388 // control signals to the datapath. These are not registered here, as they end 389 // at registers in the datapath. 390 391 always @(*) begin 392 data_out_wren = (load == 1'b1) || (flow == 1'b1) || (flush == 1'b1) || (dump == 1'b1) || (pass == 1'b1); 393 data_buffer_wren = (fill == 1'b1) || (dump == 1'b1) || (pass == 1'b1); 394 use_buffered_data = (flush == 1'b1) || (dump == 1'b1) || (pass == 1'b1); 395 end 396 397 endmodule 398 399 // For a 64-bit connection, the resulting skid buffer uses 128 registers for 400 // the buffers, 4 to 9 registers (and associated LUTs) for the FSM and 401 // interface outputs, depending on the particular state encoding chosen by the 402 // CAD tool, and easily reaches a high operating speed. 403 404

Tools Used To Write This Book

  • v2h.py, which converts commented Verilog files into HTML files. Comments are processed as Markdown. Thus, the web page doesn't get stale as the code evolves.
  • verilinter, which is a small script using Verilator and Icarus Verilog to lint Verilog-2001 files. This little tool catches a lot of simple and not so simple Verilog mistakes and corner cases.
  • Intel Quartus Prime. The CAD tool suite for Intel (ex-Altera) FPGAs. It's smaller and easier to run than Xilinx Vivado, and produces excellent post-elaboration and post-synthesis diagrams, which are very useful for debugging designs.
  • The particular look-and-feel of this site comes from the style.css file. It's pretty basic, but makes sure all pages look the same on pretty much any browser.
Back to FPGA Design Elements

1	
2	//# Clock Domain Crossing (CDC) Bit Synchronizer
3	
4	// Use a synchronizer to convert changes in a signal in one clock domain into
5	// changes which are synchronous to another clock domain, while reducing the
6	// probability of metastable events propagating.
7	
8	//## A Warning
9	
10	// A synchronizer is a trivial circuit, but it breaks the conventions of
11	// synchronous logic while doing its best to contain the consequences. Thus,
12	// it is very easy to use a synchronizer incorrectly, leading to worse timing
13	// closure at best, or at worst intermittent circuit malfunctions which won't
14	// be visible (or even reproducible!) in simulations. We go over the necessary
15	// subtleties in the text and code below.
16	
17	//## Relative Clock Frequency Constraints
18	
19	// This synchronizer is the fundamental building block of all other CDC
20	// circuits, and it must be used under one major constraint to operate
21	// properly: Any change on the input signal must hold steady for a **minimum**
22	// of 1.5x longer than the period of the receiving clock to guarantee enough
23	// time for three receiving clock edges (posedge/negedge/posedge, or
24	// negedge/posedge/negedge) to pass, which guarantees that the input signal
25	// will be properly sampled by a posedge.
26	
27	// Any less time, and the input signal could change back before it was sampled
28	// by the receiving clock. In other words, assuming single-cycle changes
29	// (pulses), the receiving clock domain can have a clock frequency of at most
30	// 0.66x that of the sending clock domain. For any higher receiving clock
31	// freqency, you must guarantee that the change on the input signal will last
32	// enough input clock cycles to be seen by at least three consecutive
33	// receiving clock edges.
34	
35	// If you cannot guarantee the duration of a pulse and/or have no knowledge of
36	// the relative clock domain frequencies, use a [Pulse
37	// Synchronizer](./CDC_Pulse_Synchronizer_2phase.html).
38	
39	// Similarly, if the rise/fall time of the input signal is longer than the
40	// receiving clock period, the receiving clock will sample the transistion
41	// multiple times, which will show up as possibly multiple pulses at the
42	// output. In these cases, use a [Debouncer](./Debouncer_Low_Latency.html).
43	
44	//## Single-Bit Synchronization Only
45	
46	// Also, for reasons explained in [A Primer on Clock Domain Crossing
47	// Theory](./cdc.html), the latency of a CDC Synchronizer can vary between
48	// 1 and 3 cycles, depending on clock phase and metastability events, and so
49	// **only one signal may be synchronized at each clock domain crossing**.
50	// Using multiple CDC Synchronizers in parallel is **not deterministic** as
51	// there is no guarantee they will all have the same latency.
52	
53	// If you need to pass multiple signals (e.g.: a bus), synchronize one signal
54	// in each direction as a ready/valid handshake, and capture the other signals
55	// (held steady during synchronization) directly in the receiving clock domain
56	// once the one signal has synchronized. Remember to constrain and check the
57	// delay on the other signals. The [CDC Data
58	// Synchronizer](./CDC_Word_Synchronizer.html) implements this approach, and
59	// the [CDC Pulse Synchronizer](./CDC_Pulse_Synchronizer_2phase.html) can also
60	// be used to build similar CDC circuits.
61	
62	//## Avoid Logic Glitches
63	
64	// **You must feed a CDC Synchronizer directly from a register**, with no
65	// logic between it and the synchronizer. Otherwise, it's possible that
66	// multiple logic paths will converge to the synchronizer, and convergent
67	// logic can glitch when signals change state (we're not getting into that
68	// theory here).  Normally, a subsequent register will filter out such
69	// glitches since they settle long before the next clock edge. However,
70	// a synchronizer's unrelated and asynchronous receiving clock may just happen
71	// to sample the input when a glitch occurs, transforming that glitch into
72	// a real, and completely wrong, logic pulse in the receiving clock domain!
73	// Alternately, a high signal may glitch early to low and be missed by the
74	// receiving clock.
75	
76	//## Not Usable as I/O Registers
77	
78	// On an FPGA, you should not use an I/O register as one of the stages of
79	// a synchronizer: they are too far from the main logic fabric, and
80	// synchronizer registers must be as close together as possible (see [A Primer
81	// on Clock Domain Crossing Theory](./cdc.html)). Thus, your input or output
82	// must connect to a dedicated I/O register synchronous to the I/O clock,
83	// which in turn connects to a CDC synchronizer driven by the internal clock.
84	// **This extra I/O register also filters out any input glitches, as outlined
85	// above.**
86	
87	`default_nettype none
88	
89	module CDC_Bit_Synchronizer
90	#(
91	    parameter EXTRA_DEPTH = 0 // Must be 0 or greater
92	)
93	(
94	    input   wire    receiving_clock,
95	    input   wire    bit_in,
96	    output  reg     bit_out
97	);
98	
99	// The minimum valid synchronizer depth is 2. Add more stages if the design
100	// requires it. This usually happens near the highest operating frequencies.
101	// Consult your device datasheets.
102	
103	    localparam DEPTH = 2 + EXTRA_DEPTH;
104	
105	// For Vivado, we must specify that the synchronizer registers should be
106	// placed close together (see: UG912), and to show up as part of MTBF reports.
107	
108	// For Quartus, specify that these register must not be optimized (e.g. moved
109	// into the input register of a DSP or BRAM) and to mark them as composing
110	// a synchronizer (and so be placed close together).
111	
112	// In both cases, we also specify that the registers must not be placed in I/O
113	// register locations.
114	
115	    // Vivado
116	    (* IOB = "false" *)
117	    (* ASYNC_REG = "TRUE" *)
118	
119	    // Quartus
120	    (* useioff = 0 *)
121	    (* PRESERVE *)
122	    (* altera_attribute = "-name SYNCHRONIZER_IDENTIFICATION \"FORCED IF ASYNCHRONOUS\"" *)
123	
124	    reg sync_reg [DEPTH-1:0];
125	
126	    integer i;
127	
128	    initial begin
129	        for(i=0; i < DEPTH; i=i+1) begin
130	            sync_reg [i] = 1'b0;
131	        end
132	    end
133	
134	// Pass the bit through DEPTH registers into the receiving clock domain.
135	// Peel out the first iteration to avoid a -1 index.
136	
137	    always @(posedge receiving_clock) begin
138	        sync_reg [0] <= bit_in;
139	
140	        for(i = 1; i < DEPTH; i = i+1) begin: cdc_stages
141	            sync_reg [i] <= sync_reg [i-1]; 
142	        end
143	    end
144	
145	    always @(*) begin
146	        bit_out = sync_reg [DEPTH-1];
147	    end
148	
149	endmodule
150	
151

1	
2	//# Single-Ported RAM
3	
4	// Defines a memory, of various implementation, with one read/write (1WR)
5	// port, where a write also updates the read output.  There is no synchronous
6	// clear on the output: In Quartus at least, any register driving it cannot be
7	// retimed, and it may not be as portable.  Instead, use separate logic (e.g.:
8	// an [Annuller](./Annuller.html)) to zero-out the output down the line.
9	
10	//## Write Forwarding
11	
12	// The READ_NEW_DATA parameter control the behaviour of simultaneous reads and
13	// writes to the same address. This is the most important parameter when
14	// considering what kind of memory block the CAD tool will infer.
15	
16	// * `READ_NEW_DATA = 0` describes a memory which returns the OLD value (in the
17	// memory) on coincident read and write (no write-forwarding).  This is
18	// well-suited for LUT-based memory, such as MLABs.
19	// * `READ_NEW_DATA = 1` (or any non-zero value) describes a memory which
20	// returns NEW data (from the write) on coincident read and write, usually by
21	// inferring some surrounding write-forwarding logic.  Good for dedicated
22	// Block RAMs, such as M10K.
23	
24	// The inferred write-forwarding logic also allows the RAM to operate at
25	// higher frequency, since a read corrupted by a simultaneous write to the
26	// same address will be discarded and replaced by the write value at the
27	// output mux of the forwarding logic. Otherwise, the RAM must internally
28	// perform the write on one edge of the clock, and the read on the other,
29	// which requires a longer cycle time.
30	
31	//### Quartus
32	
33	// For Quartus, if you do not want write-forwarding, but still get the higher
34	// speed at the price of indeterminate behaviour on coincident read/writes,
35	// use "no_rw_check" as part of the RAMSTYLE (e.g.: "M10K, no_rw_check").
36	// Depending on the FPGA hardware, this may also help when returning OLD data.
37	// If that fails, add this setting to your Quartus project:
38	// `set_global_assignment -name ADD_PASS_THROUGH_LOGIC_TO_INFERRED_RAMS OFF`
39	// to disable creation of write-forwarding logic, as Quartus ignores the
40	// "no_rw_check" RAMSTYLE for M10K BRAMs.
41	
42	//### Vivado
43	
44	// Vivado uses a different mechanism to control write-forwarding: set
45	// RW_ADDR_COLLISION to "yes" to force the inference of write forwarding
46	// logic, or "no" to prevent it. Otherwise, set it to "auto".
47	
48	
49	`default_nettype none
50	
51	module RAM_Single_Port
52	#(
53	    parameter                       WORD_WIDTH          = 0,
54	    parameter                       ADDR_WIDTH          = 0,
55	    parameter                       DEPTH               = 0,
56	    parameter                       RAMSTYLE            = "",
57	    parameter                       READ_NEW_DATA       = 0,
58	    parameter                       RW_ADDR_COLLISION   = "",
59	    parameter                       USE_INIT_FILE       = 0,
60	    parameter                       INIT_FILE           = "",
61	    parameter   [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    INIT_VALUE          = 0
62	)
63	(
64	    input  wire                         clock,
65	    input  wire                         wren,
66	    input  wire     [ADDR_WIDTH-1:0]    addr,
67	    input  wire     [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    write_data,
68	    output reg      [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    read_data
69	);
70	
71	    initial begin
72	        read_data = {WORD_WIDTH{1'b0}};
73	    end
74	
75	// Set the ram style to control implementation.
76	// See your CAD tool documentation for available options.
77	
78	    (* ramstyle             = RAMSTYLE *)           // Quartus
79	    (* ram_style            = RAMSTYLE *)           // Vivado
80	    (* rw_addr_collision    = RW_ADDR_COLLISION *)  // Vivado
81	
82	// This is the RAM array proper. Not how we access or implement it.
83	
84	    reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] ram [DEPTH-1:0];
85	
86	// The only difference selected by READ_NEW_DATA is the use of
87	// blocking/non-blocking assignments.  Blocking assignments make the write
88	// happen logically before the read, as ordered here, and thus describe
89	// write-forwarding behaviour where the read takes data from the write port.
90	// Non-blocking assignments make read and write take effect simultaneously at
91	// the end of the always block, so the read takes its data from the memory.
92	
93	// **UPDATE:** Since I originally wrote this code, over 10 years ago, I have
94	// learnt that using blocking assignments inside a clocked always block may
95	// cause simulation race conditions, depending on the order of evaluation of
96	// the always blocks (see IEEE Standard 1364-2001, Section 5, "Scheduling
97	// Semantics"). However, I have never seen such race conditions happen,
98	// possibly because of how this and other code was written (e.g.: it's in its
99	// own module, which affects event scheduling). I also could not find
100	// a simpler and cleaner way to express "write-before-read" behaviour while
101	// leaving the CAD tool free to infer RAM as it could. Finally, FPGA Block
102	// RAMs, and their CAD tools, have since changed and may no longer infer
103	// memory in the same way or have exactly the same supported behaviours in
104	// hardware.  This module should be sufficient for most purposes, but check
105	// your synthesis results! 
106	
107	    generate
108	        // Returns OLD data
109	        if (READ_NEW_DATA == 0) begin
110	            always @(posedge clock) begin
111	                if(wren == 1'b1) begin
112	                    ram[addr] <= write_data;
113	                end
114	                read_data <= ram[addr];
115	            end
116	        end
117	        // Returns NEW data
118	        // This isn't proper, but the CAD tool expects it for inference.
119	        // verilator lint_off BLKSEQ
120	        else begin
121	            always @(posedge clock) begin
122	                if(wren == 1'b1) begin
123	                    ram[addr] = write_data;
124	                end
125	                read_data = ram[addr];
126	            end
127	        end
128	        // verilator lint_on BLKSEQ
129	    endgenerate
130	
131	// If you are not using an init file, the following code will set all memory
132	// locations to INIT_VALUE. The CAD tool should generate a memory
133	// initialization file from that.  This is useful to cleanly implement small
134	// collections of registers (e.g.: via RAMSTYLE = "logic" on Quartus), without
135	// having to deal with an init file.  Your CAD tool may complain about too
136	// many for-loop iterations if your memory is very deep. Adjust the tool
137	// settings to allow more loop iterations.
138	
139	// At a minimum, the initialization file format is one value per line, one for
140	// each memory word from 0 to DEPTH-1, in bare hexadecimal (e.g.: 0012 to init
141	// a 16-bit memory word with 16'h12). Note that if your WORD_WIDTH isn't
142	// a multiple of 4, the CAD tool may complain about the width mismatch.
143	// You can base yourself on this Python [memory initialization file
144	// generator](./RAM_generate_empty_init_file.py).
145	
146	    generate
147	        if (USE_INIT_FILE == 0) begin
148	            integer i;
149	            initial begin
150	                for (i = 0; i < DEPTH; i = i + 1) begin
151	                    ram[i] = INIT_VALUE;
152	                end
153	            end
154	        end
155	        else begin
156	            initial begin
157	                $readmemh(INIT_FILE, ram);
158	            end
159	        end
160	    endgenerate
161	
162	endmodule
163	
164

content.thinking: I now have a thorough understanding of the project. Let me look at a couple more to round out understanding of categories: a function file (clog2_function.vh), an arithmetic module (Counter_Binary.v), and the tools generate_instance.py and verilinter. Let me also check the tests directory file and the .core file briefly. Let me batch these.

content.content:
1	
2	//# Ceiling of log<sub>2</sub>(N) function
3	
4	// Taken from the Verilog-2001 Language Reference Manual (LRM) standard example
5	// since $clog2() doesn't exist prior to Verilog-2005 (and thus,
6	// SystemVerilog).
7	
8	// This returns the necessary number of bits to index N items with a binary
9	// integer. For example:
10	
11	// * clog2(15) returns 4
12	// * clog2(16) returns 4
13	// * clog2(17) returns 5
14	// * etc...
15	
16	// Bring in the function at the start of the body of your module like so:
17	
18	//    `include "clog2_function.vh"
19	
20	// Pass the function a value which, at elaboration time, is either a constant
21	// or an expression which evaluates to a constant. Then use that value as an
22	// integer for a localparam, genvar, etc...
23	
24	// You don't need this function often, but it's very handy when a module
25	// receives some item count as a parameter, and you need to create an internal
26	// register to hold an index to those items (e.g.: a binary counter).
27	
28	// We use a temp value for calculations since Vivado raises warnings if we
29	// internally assign a value to a function input port.
30	
31	// Since this is an included file, it must be idempotent. (defined only once globally)
32	
33	`ifndef CLOG2_FUNCTION
34	`define CLOG2_FUNCTION
35	
36	function integer clog2;
37	    input integer value;
38	          integer temp;
39	    begin
40	        temp = value - 1;
41	        for (clog2 = 0; temp > 0; clog2 = clog2 + 1) begin
42	            temp = temp >> 1;
43	        end
44	    end
45	endfunction
46	
47	`endif
48	
49

1	
2	//# A Binary Up/Down Counter
3	
4	// This counter counts by the `INCREMENT` parameter value, up or down, when
5	// `run` is high.  Set `up_down` to 0 to count up, or to 1 to count down.
6	// Load overrides counting, so you can load a `load_count` value even if `run`
7	// is low.  `clear` puts the counter back at INITIAL_COUNT.  The counter will
8	// wrap around if it goes below zero or above `(2^WORD_WIDTH)-1` and set
9	// `overflow` for that cycle.
10	
11	// The `INCREMENT` parameter allows you to deal with situations where, for
12	// example, you need to count the number of bytes transferred, but your
13	// interface transfers multiple bytes per cycle.
14	
15	// When chaining counters, which may happen if you are counting in unusual
16	// bases where each digit has its own counter, AND the `carry_out` of the
17	// previous counter with the signal fed to the `run` input of the next
18	// counter. The `carry_in` is kept for generality, as are the `carries` into
19	// each bit.
20	
21	`default_nettype none
22	
23	module Counter_Binary
24	#(
25	    parameter                   WORD_WIDTH      = 0,
26	    parameter [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]  INCREMENT       = 0,
27	    parameter [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]  INITIAL_COUNT   = 0
28	)
29	(
30	    input   wire                        clock,
31	    input   wire                        clear,
32	
33	    input   wire                        up_down, // 0/1 --> up/down
34	    input   wire                        run,
35	
36	    input   wire                        load,
37	    input   wire    [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    load_count,
38	
39	    input   wire                        carry_in,
40	    output  wire                        carry_out,
41	    output  wire    [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    carries,
42	    output  wire                        overflow,
43	
44	    output  wire    [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    count
45	);
46	
47	    localparam WORD_ZERO = {WORD_WIDTH{1'b0}};
48	
49	// First we calculate the next counter value using a [Binary
50	// Adder/Subtractor](./Adder_Subtractor_Binary.html). Having a dedicated
51	// module for this allows us to change how the counter works (e.g.: BCD or
52	// other counting schemes) without altering any other logic. It also hides the
53	// tricks needed for correct arithmetic logic inference.
54	
55	    wire [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] incremented_count;
56	    wire                  carry_out_internal;
57	    wire [WORD_WIDTH-1:0] carries_internal;
58	    wire                  overflow_internal;
59	
60	    Adder_Subtractor_Binary
61	    #(
62	        .WORD_WIDTH (WORD_WIDTH)
63	    )
64	    calc_next_count
65	    (
66	        .add_sub    (up_down), // 0/1 -> A+B/A-B
67	        .carry_in   (carry_in),
68	        .A          (count),
69	        .B          (INCREMENT),
70	        .sum        (incremented_count),
71	        .carry_out  (carry_out_internal),
72	        .carries    (carries_internal),
73	        .overflow   (overflow_internal)
74	    );
75	
76	// Then calculate which value to load into the counter, and when.
77	
78	    reg [WORD_WIDTH-1:0]    next_count      = WORD_ZERO;
79	    reg                     load_counter    = 0;
80	    reg                     clear_counter   = 0;
81	    reg                     load_flags      = 0;
82	    reg                     clear_flags     = 0;
83	
84	    always @(*) begin
85	        next_count      = (load  == 1'b1) ? load_count : incremented_count;
86	        load_counter    = (run   == 1'b1) || (load == 1'b1);
87	        clear_counter   = (clear == 1'b1);
88	        load_flags      = (run   == 1'b1);
89	        clear_flags     = (load  == 1'b1) || (clear == 1'b1);
90	    end
91	
92	// Finally, store the next count value and flags, using a [Register](./Register.html).
93	
94	    Register
95	    #(
96	        .WORD_WIDTH     (WORD_WIDTH),
97	        .RESET_VALUE    (INITIAL_COUNT)

#! /usr/bin/python3

"""
Reads in a file which defines a Verilog module and outputs a ready-to-populate
instantiation of that module. **This is a hack, not a real parser!** This
script only works with the fixed module definition style of the FPGA Design
Elements. For example, parameter and port block delimiters, such as "#(" and ")",
MUST be on their own lines.

When your code is very modular, instantiating the modules becomes a large
fraction of writing new code. This script automates 99% of the work of
converting a module definition header into a module instance.  You'll have to
align columns and remove any comments you don't want to keep as notes.

Simply call the script with the file name containing the module you want to
use, and pipe the output into your text editor. The instance is indented one
tabstop as that's the needed indentation almost always.

Copyright (c) 2020 Charles Eric LaForest
License: https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT
"""

import sys

# Spaces per indent level

tabstop = 4

# Some line test functions. Makes writing later code easier.

def is_comment(line):
    return line.strip().startswith("//")

def is_blank(line):
    """If a line is only spaces or tabs and a return, it's blank."""
    return line.strip(" \t") == "\n"

def is_eof(line):
    """readline only ever returns an empty line at EOF"""
    return line == ""

def is_name(line):
    return line.strip().startswith("module")

def is_parameter(line):
    return line.strip().startswith("parameter")

def is_port(line):
    line = line.strip()
    return line.startswith("input") or line.startswith("output") or line.startswith("inout")

# We read the file line by line, in a forward only manner.
# Each sub-loop eats input and appends its output until it is done.
# Each sub-loop always finishes by reading in one line to prime the next loop.
# When we reach the end of the module definition header, we are done.
# The rest of the file is never read.

if __name__ == "__main__":
    filename    = sys.argv[1]
    raw_file    = open(filename)

    instance = []

    # Skip over comments until the code
    line = raw_file.readline()
    while not is_eof(line):
        if is_comment(line) or is_blank(line):
            line = raw_file.readline()
            continue
        else:
            break

    # Module name
    while not is_eof(line):
        if is_name(line):
            line = line.strip().split()
            name = line[-1]
            name = "\t{}".format(name)
            instance.append(name)
            line = raw_file.readline()
            break
        else:
            line = raw_file.readline()
            continue

    # Skip the parameter template section if there are no parameters
    # This assumes the parameters begin IMMEDIATELY with a "#(" line
    # Else there must be NO text before the instance name

    if line.strip().startswith("#("):
        # Begin parameter block
        instance.append("\t#(")

        # Module parameters
        # With special case to handle trailing comments (often used as notes)
        while not is_eof(line):
            if line.strip().startswith(")"):
                break
            if is_comment(line) or is_blank(line):
                instance.append("\t\t{}".format(line.strip()))
                line = raw_file.readline()
                continue
            if is_parameter(line):
                line = line.strip().split()
                comment_pos = 0
                comment = ""
                if "//" in line:
                    comment_pos = line.index("//")
                    comment = " ".join(line[comment_pos:])
                equals_index = line.index("=")
                parameter = line[equals_index-1]
                # Does the parameter have a comma at the end?
                if line[equals_index+1][-1] == ",":
                    parameter = "\t\t.{}\t(),".format(parameter)
                else:
                    parameter = "\t\t.{}\t()".format(parameter)
                if comment_pos != 0:
                    parameter = parameter + "\t{}".format(comment)
                instance.append(parameter)
                line = raw_file.readline()
                continue
            else:
                line = raw_file.readline()
                continue

        # End parameter block
        instance.append("\t)")

    # Put instance name placeholder, and start port block
    instance.append("\tinstance_name\n\t(")

    # Module ports
    # With special case to handle trailing comments (often used as notes)
    while not is_eof(line):
        if line.strip().startswith(");"):
            break
        if is_comment(line) or is_blank(line):
            instance.append("\t\t{}".format(line.strip()))
            line = raw_file.readline()
            continue
        if is_port(line):
            line = line.strip().split()
            comment_pos = 0
            comment = ""
            if "//" in line:
                comment_pos = line.index("//")
                comment = " ".join(line[comment_pos:])
            port = line[comment_pos-1]
            # Does the port have a comma at the end?
            port_bare = port.strip(",")
            if port[-1] == ",":
                port = "\t\t.{}\t(),".format(port_bare)
            else:
                port = "\t\t.{}\t()".format(port_bare)
            if comment_pos != 0:
                port = port + "\t{}".format(comment)
            instance.append(port)
            line = raw_file.readline()
            continue
        else:
            line = raw_file.readline()
            continue

    # End port block, which ends the module instance
    instance.append("\t);")

    # No need to read more from the file
    raw_file.close()

    # Convert tabs to spaces and print
    # Except for blank lines (just delete the tabs instead)
    # There are no "\n" at this point (it's a list of strings)
    # FIXME: we could do some work here to line things up dynamically.
    for line in instance:
        if line.lstrip("\t") == "":
            line = ""
        else:
            line = line.expandtabs(tabstop)
        print(line)

1. **Verilog Coding Standard** (verilog.html) — the restricted Verilog-2001 style
2. **System Design Standard** (system.html) — modularization, Core/Instance/Adapter/Shim layering

5. **FSM implementation** (fsm.html) — the COTTC methodology, with Skid Buffer as canonical example
6. **The toolchain** (v2h.py, generate_instance.py, generate_file_skeleton.py, verilinter, FuseSoC .core)
7. **Module categories**: Boolean Logic, Synchronous Logic (Register, RAM), Integer Arithmetic, Pulse Logic, Elastic Pipelines, Arbitration, CDC, Interfaces

The modules build on each other (Counter uses Adder_Subtractor and Register; Skid Buffer uses Register; etc.), forming a clear dependency hierarchy.

Now let me design the tutorial. The project is large (150+ modules), so a medium-to-large tutorial (20-30 lectures) is appropriate. But the user note says "逻辑优先，不要为了数量硬拆". I should design a coherent path from zero to expert.

Let me structure into units following the three-layer principle:

**Unit 1 (beginner): 项目概览与阅读方式** — What is this book, how to read/use it, directory structure, how to run/clone, the "modules not usable as-is" design decision, v2h.py documentation toolchain.

**Unit 2 (beginner): Verilog 编码规范基础** — The Verilog-2001 coding standard: default_nettype none, reg/wire, parameterization, blocking/non-blocking, ternary vs if/else, resets (last-assignment-wins), Register module. This is foundational — all modules follow it.

**Unit 3 (beginner): 系统设计标准与模块化** — system.html: modularization, Core/Instance/Adapter/Shim, separating data/control/interface, building block libraries, constraints.

**Unit 5 (intermediate): 同步寄存器与流水线寄存器** — Register, Register_areset, Register_Toggle, Register_Pipeline, Register_Pipeline_Variable. Storage primitives that everything uses.

**Unit 6 (intermediate): 存储器 (RAM)** — RAM_Single_Port, RAM_Simple_Dual_Port, RAM_True_Dual_Port, RAM_Multiported_LE, RAM_1WnR_Replicated. Memory inference, write-forwarding, init files.

**Unit 7 (intermediate): 整数算术构件** — Adder_Subtractor_Binary, CarryIn, Arithmetic_Predicates, Counter_Binary, Width_Adjuster. How arithmetic is composed from building blocks.

**Unit 8 (intermediate): Ready/Valid 握手原理** — handshake.html theory: interfaces, handshake procedure, deadlocks/livelocks, delayed handshakes, underlying synchronization.

**Unit 9 (intermediate): 弹性流水线基础 (Skid Buffer & FSM)** — Pipeline_Skid_Buffer as the canonical example: the FSM COTTC methodology, data/control separation, state encoding.

**Unit 10 (intermediate): 弹性流水线构件家族** — Pipeline_Half_Buffer, Pipeline_FIFO_Buffer, Pipeline_Credit_Buffer, Fork/Join, Merge (arbiters), Gate, Stall_Smoother. Composing elastic pipelines.

**Unit 11 (intermediate): 仲裁与同步** — Priority_Encoder, Arbiter_Priority, Arbiter_Round_Robin, Synchronous_Muller_C_Element.

**Unit 12 (intermediate): 时钟域穿越 (CDC) 理论与基础** — cdc.html theory + CDC_Bit_Synchronizer, Reset_Synchronizer, CDC_Flag_Bit.

**Unit 13 (advanced): CDC 数据与脉冲同步** — CDC_Word_Synchronizer, CDC_Pulse_Synchronizer (2/4-phase), Weinstein_Flancter, CDC_FIFO_Buffer.
